name: 17α-Estradiol (Men)
one_liner: Stereoisomer of estradiol that extends male mouse lifespan without feminization. No human data.
plain_summary: 17α-estradiol is a chemical mirror-image of the female hormone estradiol that, in mice, dramatically extends male lifespan without any feminizing effects. It's one of the most exciting findings from the NIA Interventions Testing Program. The catch: zero human trials. Don't take this. Watch the science.
cost_signal: Not commercially available legitimately. Compounded versions $50–$200/month from unregulated sources — not recommended.
who_should_consider: Nobody, currently. Listed for completeness because it shows up in longevity podcasts.
who_should_skip: Everyone, until human data exists. Especially: hormone-sensitive cancer history, anyone considering self-medication.
verdict: Promising mouse data, zero human data. Don't take this yet. Watch for human trials.
what_to_do: Don't take this. There is no validated human protocol or dose.
If you encounter clinics offering it: walk away. The compounded versions have unstudied pharmacokinetics and contamination risks.
Watch the literature: search 'alpha-estradiol clinical trial' periodically. If a Phase 2 reads out positively, revisit.
If you want to bet on the same general pathway (mTOR), rapamycin has more data and an off-label path.
side_effects: Theoretical: estrogenic effects at high doses

name: Acarbose
one_liner: Carbohydrate-blocker with strong mouse-lifespan signal. Limited human longevity data.
plain_summary: Acarbose is a cheap diabetes drug that slows how fast your gut absorbs starches and sugars from a meal — flattening the blood-sugar spike afterward. In mice it consistently extends lifespan. In humans the longevity case is plausible but not proven. The catch: it ferments undigested carbs in your gut, which causes a lot of gas. That side effect is why most people who could benefit don't take it.
cost_signal: $15–$40/month generic.
who_should_consider: People with pre-diabetes, post-meal blood sugar spikes, or anyone who eats a high-carb diet and wants to smooth the metabolic impact without changing food.
who_should_skip: Inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's, UC). Severe kidney impairment. History of bowel obstruction. Anyone who can't tolerate gas/bloating.
verdict: Quietly intriguing. Strong mouse data, plausible human benefit via postprandial glucose smoothing, low cost. Underused. GI tolerability is the practical barrier.
what_to_do: Talk to your doctor; this is prescription. Easier to get if you have pre-diabetes or insulin resistance.
Start tiny: 25 mg with the first bite of one meal a day. Increase slowly.
Target dose: 50–100 mg with each carb-containing meal.
Take it with food, NOT before. Skip it if the meal is very low-carb (no benefit).
Expect significant gas/bloating in the first weeks. It usually improves; if it doesn't, the drug isn't for you.
Avoid if you have IBD, severe kidney disease, or any history of bowel obstruction.
If you have hypoglycemia while on insulin or sulfonylureas, treat with glucose tablets, NOT sucrose (acarbose blocks sucrose absorption).

name: ACE Inhibitors / ARBs
one_liner: Hypertension treatment with mortality benefit and renal protection. Modest direct longevity signal.
plain_summary: If your blood pressure is high, ACE inhibitors and ARBs (ramipril, lisinopril, telmisartan, valsartan) are workhorse drugs that lower it AND protect your kidneys and heart muscle. The mortality benefit comes mostly from blood-pressure control — but for the same BP reduction, these classes outperform some others on long-term outcomes.
cost_signal: $5–$20/month generic.
who_should_consider: Anyone with hypertension (BP >130/80), diabetes with proteinuria, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
who_should_skip: Pregnancy. Bilateral renal artery stenosis. History of angioedema on ACE inhibitor.
verdict: If you have hypertension, this is one of the highest-value interventions you can make. The direct longevity benefit beyond BP control is modest but real. Combined with statins, ACEi/ARBs are the workhorses of preventive cardiology.
what_to_do: If your BP is consistently above 130/80, get a workup. Don't just live with high BP.
First-line options: ramipril (5–10 mg), lisinopril (10–40 mg), telmisartan (40–80 mg). All once daily.
If you get a dry persistent cough on ACE inhibitor (~10% of people), switch to an ARB (sartan ending). Same benefits, no cough.
Take it the same time every day. Bedtime dosing has slight evidence for cardiovascular benefit (the 'Hygia' study).
Get blood work at 2–4 weeks after starting and after dose changes: potassium and creatinine.
Stop immediately if you become pregnant or are trying to conceive — these are teratogenic.
Don't combine ACE inhibitor + ARB. Pick one.
side_effects: Renal function decline (in renal artery stenosis)

name: Aged Garlic Extract
one_liner: Garlic extract aged 18-20 months. Multiple meta-analyses show modest but consistent BP reduction. Safer than raw garlic at therapeutic doses.
plain_summary: Garlic extract that's been aged in alcohol-water for 18-20 months, which makes it gentler and dose-standardized. Studies of about 1,000 people across 20 trials show it lowers blood pressure by 5/2.5 mmHg — about half the effect of a typical BP medication. One smaller study also showed it slowed buildup of dangerous coronary plaque over a year.
cost_signal: $15-30/month for Kyolic AGE. Other 'aged' brands cheaper but less data.
who_should_consider: Borderline or stage-1 hypertension and you're not yet on medication
On BP meds but not at target — adjunct therapy
Want a low-risk add-on to a heart-health protocol
who_should_skip: On anticoagulants (warfarin, DOACs)
Already at target BP
Pre-surgery within 7 days
Severe hypertension — get on real BP medication first
verdict: Modest BP reduction with consistent evidence across 20+ RCTs. Smaller effect than first-line BP meds but no prescription needed and safer than raw garlic. Worth considering if your BP is borderline or as adjunct to first-line therapy. Eating raw garlic gives you some of the same compounds but in less reproducible doses.
what_to_do: Aged garlic extract (Kyolic AGE — the most-studied brand): 600-1200 mg/day in divided doses with food. Take consistently for at least 12 weeks before evaluating BP effect. Discontinue 7 days before surgery (mild antiplatelet effect).
side_effects: Garlic breath/body odor (minimal with aged extract vs raw)

name: Alpha-Lipoic Acid (ALA)
one_liner: Modest evidence for diabetic neuropathy at high doses (600mg+). General-purpose 'antioxidant' marketing exceeds the actual longevity evidence. Consider only for specific neuropathy indication.
plain_summary: Alpha-lipoic acid (ALA) is a fatty acid your body makes naturally and that's also sold as a supplement. It's a coenzyme involved in mitochondrial energy production and has antioxidant properties. The strongest evidence is for diabetic peripheral neuropathy — high-dose IV ALA in the SYDNEY trials produced symptom improvement, and oral 600mg/day showed modest benefit in subsequent studies. Beyond neuropathy, the marketing exceeds the evidence: general antioxidant claims, weight loss, longevity benefits — all of these are weak or null in real RCTs. If you have diabetic neuropathy, it's worth discussing with your prescriber. For general 'longevity' use, the evidence isn't there.
who_should_consider: Adults with diagnosed diabetic peripheral neuropathy, especially symptomatic, who want to add an evidence-supported adjunct to first-line treatments. Possibly: chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (preliminary evidence only).
who_should_skip: Healthy adults seeking generic 'longevity' or 'antioxidant' benefits — evidence is weak. Adults on diabetes medications without monitoring (ALA can lower blood glucose, additive hypoglycemia risk). Pregnancy (limited safety data). Children (limited data). Anyone with thiamine deficiency — high-dose ALA may worsen symptoms; correct thiamine first.
verdict: Skip unless you have diabetic peripheral neuropathy and want to discuss adding it to first-line treatment. The general-population 'antioxidant longevity' use case lacks trial support. The neuropathy evidence is modest — short-term symptomatic improvement, less convincing long-term progression effect. Sodium R-lipoate is more bioavailable than racemic. For general longevity goals, your money is better spent elsewhere.
what_to_do: If considering for diabetic neuropathy: discuss 600-1800mg/day oral with prescriber. Take on empty stomach (food reduces absorption ~30%). Sodium R-lipoate is more bioavailable than racemic R/S form. Trial typically lasts 3-6 months before assessing benefit. Not first-line for neuropathy — gabapentin, duloxetine, and pregabalin have stronger evidence. For general 'antioxidant' or longevity use: skip. The data doesn't support it.
contraindications: Pregnancy (insufficient data). Severe thiamine deficiency. Severe diabetes without close monitoring (additive hypoglycemia). Active liver disease.
side_effects: GI: nausea, vomiting (more common at higher doses or with food on empty stomach). Mild hypoglycemia in diabetics. Skin rash. Insulin autoimmune syndrome reported rarely (mostly Asian populations).

name: Ashwagandha
one_liner: Adaptogenic herb (Withania somnifera) with consistent small-to-moderate RCT evidence for reducing stress and cortisol. Not a longevity drug.
plain_summary: An adaptogenic herb from Indian traditional medicine. Studies of about 400 people across 5 trials consistently show 300-600mg/day reduces stress scores and lowers cortisol by 14-32% over 6-12 weeks. Effect is real and moderate — bigger than most supplements but smaller than prescription anxiolytics. Important: this is a stress and sleep supplement, not a longevity drug.
cost_signal: $15-25/month for KSM-66 or Sensoril branded extract.
who_should_consider: Chronic stress with elevated cortisol symptoms (belly fat, poor sleep, anxiety)
You want a non-prescription anxiolytic alternative
Sleep issues driven by stress/rumination
who_should_skip: Pregnant or trying to conceive — avoid
Hyperthyroid or autoimmune thyroid disease
On sedatives or sleep medications (additive effect)
You have a history of liver issues
verdict: Real evidence for reducing stress and cortisol. Effect size moderate, comparable to mild anxiolytics. Not a longevity drug — short-term symptom relief, not life extension. Worth considering if chronic stress is degrading your sleep and decision-making. Cycle off every few months pending long-term safety data.
what_to_do: Standardized root extract (KSM-66 or Sensoril): 300-600 mg/day with food. Effect on stress takes 2-4 weeks. Cycle 8-12 weeks on, 4 weeks off until long-term safety data exists. Monitor for jaundice, fatigue, or upper-right-quadrant pain (rare liver toxicity).

name: Astaxanthin
one_liner: Powerful carotenoid from microalgae. Modest evidence for skin elasticity, eye fatigue, and lipid oxidation. Marketed beyond what the data supports.
plain_summary: A red pigment from microalgae (it's what makes salmon and shrimp pink). Small studies show 4-12mg/day improves skin elasticity slightly and reduces eye fatigue from screens. The marketing claims (anti-aging, performance, etc.) are way ahead of the actual evidence — this is a quality-of-life supplement, not a life-extension drug.
cost_signal: $15-30/month for 4-12mg algae-derived astaxanthin.
who_should_consider: Visible photo-aging on skin and you've already done sunscreen + retinoids
Significant screen-related eye fatigue
You don't eat salmon/shrimp/wild-caught fish (natural sources)
who_should_skip: You want longevity benefit — no human mortality data
Budget-constrained — sunscreen, retinoid, and dietary salmon cost less and do more
You eat 2+ servings wild salmon/week — already getting it
verdict: Real but small effects on skin and eye fatigue. Not a longevity drug — marketing is way ahead of evidence. If you want the antioxidant effect, eat salmon (the natural source) or a serving of red-pigmented algae. Skip the expensive supplements unless skin/eye fatigue is your specific issue.
what_to_do: If you want to try: 4-12 mg/day of astaxanthin from microalgae source (Haematococcus pluvialis). Take with a meal containing fat for absorption (salmon, eggs, olive oil). Effect on skin and eye fatigue takes 8-12 weeks to appear.
side_effects: Mild orange-red skin tint at very high doses (rare)

name: Berberine
one_liner: 'Nature's metformin' — real glucose-lowering effect, no longevity outcome data.
plain_summary: Berberine is a plant alkaloid sometimes called 'nature's metformin' — it really does lower blood sugar, comparable to a moderate dose of metformin. It also lowers LDL cholesterol modestly. There are no human longevity outcome trials. Reasonable alternative if you want metformin's effects but can't or won't take a prescription drug.
who_should_consider: People with pre-diabetes or mild insulin resistance who can't or won't take metformin. Useful as a metabolic adjunct.
who_should_skip: Pregnancy, lactation. Anyone on multiple prescription meds without checking interactions. People with strong serotonergic medications (theoretical concern).
verdict: Real glucose-lowering effect. If you have prediabetes and can't or won't take metformin, this is a reasonable alternative. Not a weight-loss drug.
what_to_do: Dose: 500 mg, 2–3 times per day, taken with meals. Total 1000–1500 mg/day.
Standard berberine has poor absorption. Dihydroberberine (DHB) is more bioavailable — 200 mg DHB ≈ 500 mg berberine.
Take with food to reduce GI upset (constipation and cramping are the main complaints).

Check for drug interactions before starting — berberine inhibits the CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein systems, which affects many medications. Statins, antibiotics, blood thinners are common collisions. Pharmacist check is wise.
Cycle it: 2–3 months on, 1 month off, is a common protocol some people use to avoid theoretical tolerance.
Monitor: fasting glucose and HbA1c at 3 months to confirm it's actually doing something.
Skip during pregnancy and breastfeeding (theoretical risk to infant).

name: Blueberries and Anthocyanin-Rich Berries
one_liner: Blueberries and other dark berries are a top dietary source of anthocyanins. Modest RCT evidence for blood pressure, vascular function, and cognition.
plain_summary: Blueberries and other dark berries are loaded with anthocyanins (the deep purple/blue pigments). A 6-month trial showed that one cup of blueberries a day modestly improved blood vessel function and lowered blood pressure by about 5/2 mmHg. Long-term cohort studies link high berry consumption to ~32% lower heart attack risk in younger women. Skip the supplements — eat the actual berries.
cost_signal: $15-30/month for 1 cup/day of frozen mixed berries (Costco bulk best value). Fresh in season cheaper.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone — easy upgrade
Borderline BP
Family history of heart disease
Older adult wanting cognitive support
Currently low fruit intake
who_should_skip: On warfarin and considering high-dose anthocyanin supplements (food doses fine)
Sugar-restricted diets — measure portion (a cup is meaningful carbs)
verdict: Add 1 cup/day of blueberries (or mixed dark berries) to your routine. Small but real cardiovascular and possible cognitive benefit. Frozen are nutritionally equivalent and cheaper. Skip the 'anthocyanin supplement' capsules — anthocyanins from real berries come with fiber and other polyphenols that supplements miss.
what_to_do: Eat 1 cup (150g) of blueberries or other dark berries (blackberries, raspberries, blackcurrants, tart cherries) most days. Frozen is fine — same anthocyanins, cheaper, available year-round. Add to oatmeal, yogurt, or eat as a snack. Mixed berries deliver complementary anthocyanin profiles. Don't bother with 'anthocyanin extract' capsules — the food matrix matters.
contraindications: Anthocyanin supplements at high doses may interact with anticoagulants
side_effects: Mild GI upset at very high intake

name: Brazil Nuts (Selenium)
one_liner: Cheapest reliable selenium source. 1-2 nuts/day matches RDA. Don't exceed — selenium toxicity is real.
plain_summary: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that's hard to get reliably from food in many regions where soil is selenium-poor. Brazil nuts are the unusual exception — a single nut can contain 50–500 micrograms of selenium (the RDA is 55 µg, the upper safe limit is 400 µg). One or two nuts daily covers the RDA cheaply. Selenium is a cofactor for selenoproteins that protect cells from oxidative damage, support thyroid function, and regulate immune response. Don't go higher than 2-3 nuts daily — selenium toxicity (selenosis) causes hair loss, brittle nails, and neurological symptoms.
who_should_consider: Adults in selenium-poor regions (much of Europe, parts of US Midwest, China) without regular fish/shellfish intake. People with autoimmune thyroid disease (Hashimoto's): adequate selenium status moderates anti-TPO antibodies. Vegans (selenium intake from plant sources is highly variable).
who_should_skip: Anyone already taking a multivitamin or supplement with selenium (most contain 50-200 µg). People with normal/high plasma selenium (>120 µg/L) — additional intake may increase prostate cancer risk per SELECT findings. Children: dose differently and don't approach 1 nut/day.
verdict: Cheap correction for a real deficiency. 1-2 Brazil nuts daily covers the RDA reliably and uses the food matrix the body evolved with. Don't take selenium on top, don't go higher than 2 nuts. Test plasma selenium if you're uncertain or take other selenium-containing supplements. The supplementation story is U-shaped — correct deficiency, don't oversupplement.
what_to_do: 1-2 Brazil nuts per day, not more. Variability is huge — Brazilian-soil-grown nuts can have 5-10x more selenium than those grown elsewhere. If supplementing instead, 100-200 µg selenium daily (selenomethionine or selenium yeast forms preferred over selenite). DON'T take Brazil nuts AND a selenium supplement simultaneously — easy to overshoot. Test plasma selenium if uncertain (target 100-130 µg/L).
contraindications: Already taking multivitamin with selenium. Recently diagnosed prostate cancer (high-dose selenium controversial; consult oncologist). Children at adult doses.
side_effects: Excess: hair loss, brittle nails, garlic breath, GI upset, peripheral neuropathy. Reflective of plasma >150 µg/L. Recoverable on discontinuation.

name: Calcium (Food First)
one_liner: Important for bone, but more is not always better. Food-first calcium plus vitamin D and resistance training beats blind high-dose pills.
plain_summary: Calcium matters for bone health, especially after menopause and in older adults. But high-dose calcium pills are not a longevity hack, and fracture prevention depends on the whole system: protein, vitamin D, resistance training, fall prevention, and sometimes medication. Food-first is the sane default.
cost_signal: Food dependent. Supplements are cheap but often unnecessary if diet covers intake.
who_should_consider: Postmenopausal women, older adults, low-dairy/low-calcium diets, osteoporosis/osteopenia risk, low vitamin D status.
who_should_skip: History of kidney stones or hypercalcemia without clinician guidance. People already meeting intake from food.
verdict: Aim for adequate calcium, not maximal calcium. Use food first, supplement only the gap, and pair with vitamin D plus strength/impact training.
what_to_do: Estimate intake: dairy, fortified soy milk, calcium-set tofu, sardines with bones, greens, fortified foods.
Target roughly 1,000-1,200 mg/day total intake depending on age/sex.
If supplementing, fill the gap rather than taking 1,200 mg blindly.
Split supplemental calcium into <=500 mg doses with meals.
Pair with vitamin D adequacy and resistance/impact training.
contraindications: Recurrent calcium kidney stones without evaluation
side_effects: Kidney stone risk in susceptible people

name: Cocoa Flavanols
one_liner: Plant compound from cocoa with the strongest RCT evidence of any 'food' supplement for heart-disease death reduction.
plain_summary: A plant compound found in cocoa that helps your blood vessels work better. The biggest study ever done on a supplement (21,000 people over 4 years) showed people taking it had 27% fewer heart-related deaths than people taking placebo. Mild blood pressure improvement too.
cost_signal: $25-35/month for supplement-grade products (CocoaVia, similar). Generic cocoa powder is much cheaper but flavanol content varies widely.
who_should_consider: You have elevated blood pressure or family history of heart disease
You're 50+ and looking for low-risk cardiovascular hedges
You already do the basics (no smoking, exercise, statin if indicated) and want one more lever
who_should_skip: You think dark chocolate counts — it doesn't (way too little flavanol per serving, plus sugar)
You're already on optimal BP control and statin — marginal additional benefit
Budget-constrained — sauna or omega-3 likely deliver more per dollar
verdict: Worth considering for cardiovascular protection, especially if you have elevated BP or family history. The evidence is real but modest per-person. Get the supplement form (CocoaVia, ~$30/mo) — eating dark chocolate doesn't deliver enough flavanols and adds sugar.
what_to_do: Take 500-900 mg cocoa flavanols/day in supplement form (CocoaVia Cardio Health, Acticor, or equivalent). One serving per day, anytime, with or without food. Stick with it — benefit takes months and the COSMOS trial only saw effect over 3-4 years.
side_effects: Caffeine sensitivity (some products contain residual caffeine)

name: Coffee (Caffeinated)
one_liner: Daily coffee (3-5 cups) is consistently associated with lower all-cause mortality across dozens of cohort studies. Causality probable but not proven.
plain_summary: Daily coffee (3-5 cups) is one of the most consistent food/beverage patterns associated with lower mortality in observational studies — about 17% lower all-cause death rate, with strong protective signals for type 2 diabetes, Parkinson's, liver disease, and several cancers. The evidence isn't from randomized trials but the signal is unusually consistent across populations and ages. Use filtered (drip) coffee — espresso/French press at high volumes raises cholesterol slightly.
cost_signal: Free if you make at home from beans (~$10-30/month). $100+/month if you buy daily takeaway.
who_should_consider: You already drink coffee — keep going
Family history of T2D or Parkinson's
Liver-enzyme elevations (NAFLD, mild fibrosis)
Need afternoon alertness without sugar
who_should_skip: Severe anxiety disorders
Pregnancy (limit to 200mg/day caffeine)
Sleep disturbance from caffeine
Slow CYP1A2 metabolizer with hypertension
You don't drink it now — evidence isn't strong enough to start
verdict: If you already drink 2-4 cups of filtered coffee a day, you're doing one of the best-evidenced longevity practices. If you don't drink it, the evidence isn't strong enough to start. Avoid unfiltered (espresso, French press) at high volumes — raises LDL. Stop after noon if it disturbs sleep.
what_to_do: Drink 2-4 cups of filtered coffee in the morning hours. Avoid coffee after ~2 PM if you're not a known fast metabolizer (caffeine half-life ~5h, longer in slow metabolizers). Don't add 4 sugars and cream — that wipes out the metabolic benefit. Black, with milk, or with a small amount of sugar is fine. Decaf still has chlorogenic acid but loses the caffeine-related benefits.
contraindications: Slow CYP1A2 metabolizers with hypertension or CHD
side_effects: Insomnia (caffeine half-life ~5 hours, longer in slow metabolizers)

name: Cold Exposure
one_liner: Popular biohack with weak longevity evidence. Real for mood + brown adipose activation.
plain_summary: Cold plunges and ice baths are popular but the longevity evidence is weak. They genuinely improve mood, alertness, and may activate brown fat — but adding years to your life? Not proven. Fine if you enjoy the ritual. Skip the ice bath right after lifting if muscle growth is your goal — cold blunts the training response.
cost_signal: $0 (cold shower) to $5,000+ (home cold plunge tub).
who_should_consider: Healthy people who enjoy it for mood/alertness. People with mild-to-moderate depression as an adjunct.
who_should_skip: Cardiac disease. Raynaud's. Cold urticaria. Pregnancy. Anyone immediately after heavy resistance training (timing).
verdict: Fine if you enjoy it for mood/alertness. Don't expect longevity miracles. Don't ice immediately after training if hypertrophy matters to you.
what_to_do: Easiest entry: end your shower with 30–60 seconds of cold (full cold knob). Build up over weeks.
Cold plunge if you want it: 2–5 minutes at 10–15°C / 50–60°F. Not colder, not longer — there's no extra benefit and there is extra risk.
Time it: cold exposure in the morning for the alertness boost; not within 1–2 hours of bed (it disrupts sleep onset).
Don't ice within 4–6 hours after resistance training if you train for muscle gain. The cold blunts the muscle-growth signal. (Fine after pure cardio or rest days.)
Don't cold-plunge alone if you have any cardiac history. The cold-shock response can trigger arrhythmias.
If you have Raynaud's, cold urticaria, or known cardiac disease: skip this entirely.
Brown fat activation only matters at very low temperatures and over months. Don't expect dramatic body composition effects.

name: Collagen Peptides
one_liner: Real evidence for skin and joint endpoints. No longevity outcome data.
plain_summary: Collagen peptides have real evidence for skin elasticity, joint pain reduction, and tendon repair — but as a longevity headline, it's tertiary. Don't take it instead of total dietary protein; take it on top of, or as part of, hitting your daily protein target.
who_should_consider: Adults over 40 worried about skin or joints. People with osteoarthritis. Athletes recovering from tendon injuries.
who_should_skip: People allergic to the source (bovine, marine, porcine).
verdict: If you care about skin/joints, collagen has real evidence. As a longevity headline, it's tertiary. Don't substitute for total dietary protein.
what_to_do: Dose: 5–15 g/day. Most studies use 10 g.
Mix into coffee, tea, smoothie, oatmeal, soup. It's nearly tasteless and dissolves easily in liquids.
Take it consistently for at least 3 months before judging effects (skin and joint matrix turn over slowly).
Pair with vitamin C — it's a cofactor for collagen synthesis. A glass of OJ or a kiwi works.
For tendon repair specifically: time it 30–60 min before rehab/strength work — gelatin/collagen + vitamin C preload boosts tendon collagen synthesis.
Don't use collagen as your only protein source — it lacks tryptophan and isn't complete. Aim for 1.6 g/kg total protein from varied sources.
Marine, bovine, or porcine — all work. Pick based on diet preferences. Marine has the smallest peptide profile.

name: CoQ10 / Ubiquinol
one_liner: Mitochondrial coenzyme. Specific benefit in heart failure and statin myopathy; modest general signal.
plain_summary: CoQ10 is genuinely useful in two specific situations: heart failure and statin-induced muscle aches. For healthy adults seeking general longevity, the evidence is thin. If your doctor has you on a statin and you have muscle aches, try this before switching drugs. Otherwise, low priority.
cost_signal: $20–$50/month (ubiquinol is more expensive than ubiquinone).
who_should_consider: People on statins with muscle aches. Heart failure patients (with cardiologist).
who_should_skip: Healthy adults with no specific indication. Better evidence elsewhere on this site.
verdict: Worth taking if you have heart failure or statin-induced muscle aches. As a general longevity bet for healthy adults, evidence is thin.
what_to_do: If you're under 50 and healthy: probably skip it. Other items have better evidence.
If you're on a statin and have muscle aches: try ubiquinol 100–200 mg/day for 8 weeks. If aches improve, keep taking it. If they don't, switch statin or stop.
If you have heart failure: 100–300 mg/day of ubiquinol, after talking to your cardiologist. The Q-SYMBIO trial showed benefit.
Take it with a meal containing fat (it's fat-soluble and absorption is poor on empty stomach).
Pick ubiquinol over ubiquinone for adults over 50 — better absorbed.
If on warfarin: tell your doctor. Mild interaction; INR may need rechecking.
Effects are slow — give it 8+ weeks before judging.

name: Creatine
one_liner: Most-studied performance supplement. Real benefit for muscle, modest cognitive signal.
plain_summary: Creatine is the most-studied supplement in existence. It works: it makes you a little stronger when you train, helps you preserve muscle as you age, and there's growing evidence it helps the brain too — especially when you're sleep-deprived or vegetarian. It's cheap, safe, and the kidney-damage scare is a myth in healthy people.
cost_signal: $10–$25/month. One of the best longevity ROI on this entire site.
who_should_consider: Anyone over 40 who wants to preserve muscle and bone. Vegetarians and vegans (you get less from food). Anyone training for strength or hypertrophy.
who_should_skip: People with severe kidney disease should ask their doctor first (precautionary, evidence is reassuring in healthy kidneys).
verdict: Cheapest, most-validated supplement on this list. If you do any resistance training, take it. The longevity case is via lean mass + strength preservation, both of which are independent mortality predictors.
what_to_do: Take 3–5 grams of creatine monohydrate per day. Skip the loading phase — it just causes bloating.
Brand doesn't matter much. Look for 'Creapure' or any third-party tested product. Powder is cheapest.
Mix it into water, juice, coffee, or your protein shake. Timing doesn't matter — daily consistency does.
Drink an extra glass of water with it. Expect 1–2 lbs of water-weight gain in the first weeks (this is muscle hydration, not fat).
Combine with resistance training (2–3x/week) to get the lean-mass benefit. Creatine without lifting still helps cognition but the muscle benefit is the bigger longevity story.
Take it forever. The benefit reverses if you stop.

name: Curcumin
one_liner: Anti-inflammatory polyphenol with bioavailability problems and modest clinical effects.
plain_summary: Curcumin (the yellow compound in turmeric) has real anti-inflammatory effects, with modest clinical benefit for joint pain. The catch: it's almost completely unabsorbed unless you use a special bioavailable formulation. As a longevity headline, curcumin is overhyped. As a kitchen spice, turmeric is a healthy default.
cost_signal: $15–$40/month for enhanced-bioavailability forms. Plain curcumin is cheap but mostly wasted money.
who_should_consider: People with osteoarthritis joint pain. Anyone with chronic inflammatory conditions wanting a low-risk adjunct.
who_should_skip: On anticoagulants without doctor sign-off. Active gallstones. Pregnancy at high doses.
verdict: Modest benefit for joint pain and inflammation if you use a bioavailable form. Not a longevity headline. Eat turmeric in food (with black pepper for absorption).
what_to_do: First: cook with turmeric. Add black pepper to dishes with turmeric. Cheap, pleasant, healthy.
If supplementing: skip plain curcumin capsules. Use a bioavailability-enhanced product: Meriva (curcumin phytosome), BCM-95, or Theracurmin.
Dose for enhanced forms: 80–200 mg/day. Plain curcumin needs 1000–2000 mg+ to do anything.
Take with a fatty meal (it's fat-soluble).
Consistent 8–12 weeks before judging effect on joint pain or inflammation markers.
Stop 1–2 weeks before any surgery — may increase bleeding tendency at high doses.
Avoid if you have active gallstones (causes gallbladder contraction).
side_effects: Mild bleeding tendency at high doses

name: Dark Chocolate (cocoa flavanols)
one_liner: COSMOS trial (2022, n=21,442) is the first large RCT of cocoa flavanols on hard CV endpoints. 27% reduction in CV death.
plain_summary: Dark chocolate isn't just a guilty pleasure — high-cocoa varieties contain flavanols, plant compounds with measurable effects on blood vessels. The COSMOS trial in 2022 was the first large randomised study (over 21,000 adults) testing whether daily cocoa-flavanol supplements reduce hard cardiovascular outcomes. The headline result: a 27% reduction in cardiovascular death, despite the primary composite endpoint missing significance. Dark chocolate at the dose used in trials (≥85% cocoa, modest portion) is a reasonable food-source approach. Just watch the calories — chocolate is calorie-dense even at 85% cocoa.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated BP or family history of CV disease. People who already eat sweet desserts (replace one with dark chocolate). Anyone wanting a low-effort food addition with real evidence.
who_should_skip: Diabetes (watch portions; even 85% has some sugar). Migraines triggered by chocolate. Caffeine sensitivity at high evening intake (cocoa contains modest caffeine + theobromine). Reflux. Anyone who can't stop at one square.
verdict: One of the better-evidenced food interventions. The COSMOS trial (21,442 people, 27% reduction in CV death) is hard to wave away. Eat 20-30g of 85%+ dark chocolate daily, or use cocoa powder if you don't want the sugar. Don't switch to milk chocolate or 'dark' at 50%; the flavanol load doesn't transfer.
what_to_do: 20-30g (one or two squares) of ≥85% cocoa dark chocolate daily, OR 1 tablespoon (5g) of unsweetened cocoa powder added to oatmeal, smoothies, or coffee. Choose non-alkalised ('natural') cocoa when possible — alkalisation destroys most flavanols. Avoid milk chocolate (too little cocoa, too much sugar) and 'dark' chocolate at 50-70% cocoa (still mostly sugar). The COSMOS trial used a 500mg flavanol supplement; equivalent intake from food is roughly 30g of 85% chocolate or 1 Tbsp cocoa powder.
contraindications: Migraine triggered by chocolate. Severe sleep onset issues with evening intake. Caution in dogs (toxic) — keep out of reach.
side_effects: Calories matter at consistent intake. Caffeine/theobromine in cocoa can disturb sleep at evening intake (~25mg caffeine/30g chocolate). Can trigger reflux or migraines in susceptible people.

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q Senolytic)
one_liner: Mayo Clinic's pioneering senolytic combination. Promising mouse data, early human trials in specific diseases. NOT validated as a healthy-aging intervention. Don't try at home.
plain_summary: An experimental combination of a chemotherapy drug (dasatinib) and a natural flavonoid (quercetin) being tested at the Mayo Clinic as the first 'senolytic' — a drug class designed to kill off zombie cells that refuse to die and drive inflammation. The mouse data is exciting (mice live ~36% longer in late-life dosing). Early human trials in specific diseases (lung fibrosis, diabetic kidney disease) show signal. But dasatinib has serious side effects — pleural effusions, blood-cell drops, cardiac issues. Do NOT try this on your own.
cost_signal: Not applicable for self-purchase. Trial enrollment is the realistic access path. (Dasatinib is ~$8000/month brand, generics available for CML treatment.)
who_should_consider: Enrolled in a Mayo or other registered senolytic clinical trial
Diagnosed IPF, diabetic kidney disease, or frailty under specialist care
who_should_skip: Healthy adults trying anti-aging — risk-benefit not established
Anyone not under physician supervision
Anyone on anticoagulants, cardiac drugs, or with active cancer (other than the indication)
DIY biohackers — dasatinib is not a supplement
verdict: DO NOT self-administer. The mouse data is genuinely exciting and the Mayo trials are real, but dasatinib has serious side effects that need physician monitoring and we don't know yet if intermittent dosing in healthy people produces net benefit. If you want senolytic exposure: fisetin (already in catalogue) is the safer evidence-mining-stage choice. Quercetin alone is also safe but its solo senolytic activity is weak. Watch the trial pipeline — D+Q for healthy aging will be answered in the next 5-10 years.
what_to_do: DO NOT self-administer. Watch for trial enrollment if you have idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, diabetic kidney disease, or are eligible for the Mayo healthy-aging trials. If you want senolytic exposure now: stick with fisetin (in catalogue) which has a much better safety profile. Discuss with a longevity-focused physician if you have specific senescence-driven disease (IPF, frailty, diabetic complications).
contraindications: Anyone not enrolled in a clinical trial under physician supervision
Active cancer (unless prescribed for that specific condition)
side_effects: Dasatinib: pleural effusions (5-25%), cytopenias, fluid retention, cardiac arrhythmia, GI bleeding, severe drug interactions

name: DASH Diet
one_liner: The best-studied diet pattern for lowering blood pressure. Mediterranean-adjacent, but more explicit about sodium, potassium, and low-fat dairy.
plain_summary: DASH is the dietary pattern with the clearest blood-pressure trial evidence: vegetables, fruit, legumes, whole grains, nuts, low-fat dairy, low saturated fat, and lower sodium. It is less glamorous than Mediterranean diet, but if blood pressure is your problem, DASH is the nutrition protocol with the most direct RCT signal.
cost_signal: Usually cost-neutral. More produce and low-fat dairy; less restaurant/processed food.
who_should_consider: Anyone with elevated blood pressure, family history of hypertension, high sodium intake, or cardiometabolic risk.
who_should_skip: People with advanced kidney disease or hyperkalemia need clinician guidance because potassium-rich foods can matter.
verdict: Use DASH when blood pressure is the main nutrition target. It is one of the few diets where the named pattern itself has strong RCT evidence.
what_to_do: Build meals around vegetables, fruit, legumes, whole grains, nuts, and low-fat dairy or equivalent calcium/protein sources.
Keep sodium near 1,500-2,300 mg/day if blood pressure is high.
Use beans, potatoes, leafy greens, yogurt, fruit, and nuts to raise potassium from food.
Replace butter, cheese-heavy meals, and processed meats with olive oil, fish, legumes, poultry, yogurt, and nuts.
Track home blood pressure for 4 weeks before judging effect.
contraindications: Severe lactose intolerance if using dairy-heavy version
side_effects: GI adjustment if fiber rises quickly

name: Dietary Nitrate (Beetroot, Leafy Greens)
one_liner: Dietary nitrate from leafy greens and beetroot is converted to nitric oxide. Modest BP reduction and exercise-performance benefit. Whole-food approach beats supplements.
plain_summary: Nitrate from leafy greens (arugula, spinach, beets) is converted by bacteria in your mouth into nitric oxide — the same molecule that drugs like Viagra release. It mildly lowers blood pressure (about 4/1 mmHg) and improves exercise performance by about 3-5%. The effect is real but small. Important: mouthwash kills the bacteria that do the conversion, so harsh antiseptic mouthwash on cardio days literally undoes this.
cost_signal: Free if you're already eating vegetables. Beetroot juice (Beet It Sport): ~$15-30/month. Bag of arugula: $3-5.
who_should_consider: Borderline or stage-1 hypertension
Endurance athlete looking for legal performance boost
Anyone not eating vegetables — start here, biggest payoff per change
who_should_skip: History of calcium oxalate kidney stones (beets and spinach are high-oxalate)
You're already eating 2+ cups leafy greens daily — no benefit from supplements
You depend on harsh antiseptic mouthwash medically
verdict: Eat 1-2 cups of leafy greens daily — that's the intervention. The actual benefit comes from real food, not supplements. If you hate vegetables, beetroot juice (250mL/day) is the most concentrated source studied. Avoid antiseptic mouthwash on training days — kills the oral bacteria you need. Effect is modest but free if you're already eating vegetables.
what_to_do: Eat 1-2 cups of nitrate-rich leafy greens (arugula, spinach, swiss chard, beet greens) daily. Or drink 250-500 mL beetroot juice (Beet It Sport Shot or equivalent). For exercise benefit: take 2-3 hours before training. Avoid antiseptic mouthwash (chlorhexidine, Listerine) on training days — it kills the oral bacteria that activate nitrate.
contraindications: History of calcium oxalate kidney stones (beets and spinach are high-oxalate)
Active oral antiseptic use (kills nitrate-reducing bacteria)
side_effects: Mild GI upset from beetroot juice

name: EGCG (Green Tea)
one_liner: Green tea consumption associates with lower mortality. EGCG-only pills have safety concerns.
plain_summary: People who drink 3–5 cups of green tea a day have lower mortality in cohort studies. That benefit appears to be real. The shortcut — popping high-dose EGCG capsules — has caused liver injury in some people. Drink the tea. Skip the pills.
cost_signal: Loose-leaf green tea: $10–$30/month for daily intake. Matcha pricier.
who_should_consider: Almost anyone — tea is a healthy default beverage.
who_should_skip: Pregnant (caffeine + theoretical folate interaction at very high intake). Liver disease (extracts only). On warfarin (mild interaction).
verdict: Drink the tea. Don't pop the high-dose pills.
what_to_do: Drink 3–5 cups of green tea per day. Matcha, sencha, gyokuro all work — matcha has the highest catechin density.
Brew at 70–80°C / 160–175°F (NOT boiling) for 2–3 minutes. Boiling water destroys catechins and makes the tea bitter.
Spread across the day. Caffeine in green tea is real; switch to decaf green or skip evening cups if it disrupts sleep.
If you must supplement: limit to 200–400 mg EGCG/day, take with food, never on an empty stomach.
Stop immediately if you develop yellowing of skin/eyes, dark urine, or right-upper-belly pain — get a liver panel.
Avoid high-dose EGCG capsules entirely if you have any history of liver disease.

name: Ergothioneine
one_liner: Mushroom-derived amino acid with active uptake transporter. Strong mechanistic story; modest human data.
plain_summary: Ergothioneine is an unusual amino acid found almost exclusively in mushrooms. It has a dedicated transporter that pumps it specifically into mitochondria, where it acts as an antioxidant. The mechanism is fascinating; the human longevity data is modest. Easy answer: eat mushrooms — especially shiitake, oyster, and king oyster — a few times a week.
cost_signal: Mushrooms $5–$20/week from a grocery store. Supplement $20–$40/month.
who_should_consider: Everyone. Particularly older adults concerned about cognitive decline.
who_should_skip: Mushroom allergy (rare).
verdict: Eat mushrooms. Especially shiitake, oyster, king oyster, lion's mane. Supplement form is reasonable but mushroom intake is cheaper and more pleasant.
what_to_do: Eat mushrooms 3+ times per week. Best sources by ergothioneine content: oyster mushrooms > king oyster > shiitake > maitake > white button.
100 g of cooked oyster mushrooms provides ~5–7 mg ergothioneine. White button mushrooms are lower (~1 mg/100g) but still useful.
Cooking does not destroy ergothioneine. Sauté, roast, add to soups and stir-fries.
Lion's mane mushrooms have less ergothioneine but other interesting compounds — fine to vary.
Supplement option: 5–30 mg/day if you don't eat mushrooms. Some people just don't tolerate them.
No notable side effects or interactions reported.

name: Prolonged Fasting / Fasting-Mimicking Diet (FMD)
one_liner: Periodic 3-5 day fasting cycles. Modest metabolic + immune renewal signals.
plain_summary: Going 3–5 days on very little food (water-only, or a low-calorie low-protein 'fasting-mimicking' meal kit) once or twice a year seems to trigger a deep cellular cleanup and immune-cell renewal. Think of it like a periodic system reboot. Don't do it monthly. Don't do it without a doctor if you take medications or have any chronic condition.
cost_signal: ProLon kit ~$200/round, so $400–$800/year at quarterly cadence. DIY is much cheaper.
who_should_consider: Healthy adults curious about episodic 'metabolic stress' inputs. People with stable weight and no chronic conditions.
who_should_skip: Eating disorder history. Type 1 diabetes. Pregnancy/breastfeeding. Underweight or fragile elderly. Anyone on medications that need food. Anyone for whom 5 days of severe undereating is genuinely dangerous (most people don't know if this is them — ask a doctor).
verdict: Plausible periodic intervention. The 'training stress' analogy works: episodic, recovered from. Don't do it monthly. Quarterly is reasonable. Don't do it without medical supervision if you're on meds or have any chronic disease.
what_to_do: Easiest entry point: ProLon 5-day FMD kit (commercial, ~$200). Eat only the kit for 5 days; eat normally otherwise.
Frequency: 2–4 times per year. Quarterly is the most common protocol.
Or DIY low-cal low-protein: 600–800 kcal/day for 5 days, mostly vegetables, broth, small nuts. No animal protein.
Water fasts (no food at all) for 3–7 days have similar mechanism but harsher experience and need supervision.
Don't do prolonged fasts if you take diabetes medication (hypoglycemia risk), blood pressure meds (need adjustment), or are on warfarin/many psych meds.
Refeed slowly. Day after the fast: small meals, mostly vegetables and a little protein. Big meal day 1 = misery.
Stop early if you feel faint, get heart palpitations, or develop severe weakness.

name: Fenugreek
one_liner: Real RCT data for HbA1c reduction in T2D. Cheap, safe at typical doses. Modest effect compared to medications.
plain_summary: Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum) is a culinary spice and traditional medicine plant. Most studied for blood-sugar control: meta-analyses of RCTs in type 2 diabetes show roughly 0.5-0.9% HbA1c reduction with daily fenugreek seed powder (typically 5-10g/day). That's similar to what a low dose of metformin achieves. It's not as effective as standard medications and shouldn't replace them, but for prediabetes or as adjunct to medication it has the strongest plant-supplement evidence for glycemic control after berberine. The effect is mostly from soluble fibre and 4-hydroxyisoleucine, an unusual amino acid.
who_should_consider: Adults with prediabetes or early T2D wanting an adjunct to lifestyle. People already on metformin who want additional small effect. Anyone with elevated HbA1c (5.7-7%) for whom medication isn't yet warranted.
who_should_skip: Pregnancy (uterine stimulant traditionally used to induce labour; avoid). People with allergies to legumes/peanuts (fenugreek is botanically a legume; cross-reactivity reported). On warfarin or other anticoagulants (mild antiplatelet effect, theoretical interaction). T2D on insulin (additive hypoglycemia risk; coordinate with prescriber).
verdict: Real glycemic effect. Cheap, well-tolerated at typical doses. Modest compared to medications — about 0.5-0.9% HbA1c reduction. Reasonable for prediabetes or as cheap adjunct to metformin in early T2D. Not a substitute for medical management of established diabetes. Skip the testosterone-marketed extract products; use seed powder.
what_to_do: 5-10g fenugreek seed powder daily, with meals. Can be added to food (it's used in Indian cuisine — methi). Available as capsules; check the label for actual seed powder vs extract (extracts vary in 4-hydroxyisoleucine content). Avoid the fenugreek extract products marketed for testosterone — those are different formulations with weaker T2D evidence and stronger marketing claims. For glycemic effect, the simple seed powder is most studied.
contraindications: Pregnancy (uterine effects). Legume/peanut allergy (cross-reactivity). On insulin or sulfonylureas without prescriber awareness (additive hypoglycemia). On anticoagulants (theoretical antiplatelet interaction).
side_effects: GI: bloating, gas, diarrhea (from soluble fibre, dose-dependent). Maple-syrup body odour (sotolon excretion). Mild hypoglycemia symptoms if combined with diabetes medications.

name: Fermented Foods (yogurt, kimchi, kefir, miso, sauerkraut)
one_liner: Stanford 2021 RCT: 6 servings/day for 10 weeks increased microbiome diversity AND reduced 19 inflammation markers. The strongest microbiome-modulating intervention tested in real humans.
plain_summary: Fermented foods do something different from probiotic capsules: they deliver live microbes plus the metabolites those microbes produce while fermenting (lactic acid, peptides, polyphenol modifications). The Stanford 2021 RCT randomised adults to either a fibre-rich or fermented-foods-rich diet for 10 weeks. The fibre group barely changed; the fermented-foods group increased microbiome diversity *and* reduced 19 different inflammation markers. That combination — diversity up, inflammation down — is unusual. It's the strongest evidence for any food category as a microbiome intervention. Yogurt, kefir, kimchi, miso, sauerkraut, kombucha all qualify; daily intake matters more than which specific food.
who_should_consider: Adults wanting to address chronic low-grade inflammation, IBS, or general gut health without supplements. People recovering from courses of antibiotics. Anyone whose diet is currently low in microbial diversity (Western processed-food pattern).
who_should_skip: Severe immunocompromise (transplant patients, active chemotherapy with neutropenia, advanced HIV) — live cultures pose theoretical infection risk; medical guidance recommended. Histamine intolerance (fermented foods are high-histamine; symptoms include flushing, headaches, GI upset). Severe IBS with FODMAP-triggered symptoms (sauerkraut and kimchi can be aggravating). Salt-restricted diets (kimchi/miso/sauerkraut have ~500-1000 mg sodium per serving).
verdict: Eat fermented foods daily. Wastyk's 2021 Cell trial is unusual: a 10-week dietary intervention that produces measurable microbiome AND immune changes in healthy adults. Variety matters — yogurt + kimchi + sauerkraut hit different bacterial genera. This is one of the few food categories where the live-microbe story and the metabolite story both check out. Capsule probiotics produce smaller, more variable effects than the food form.
what_to_do: Aim for 4-6 servings daily across multiple food categories. Servings: 1 cup yogurt, 1 cup kefir, ½ cup sauerkraut, ½ cup kimchi, 1 Tbsp miso paste, 1 cup kombucha. Variety matters: different ferments deliver different microbe genera. Choose unpasteurised (raw) when safe — pasteurisation kills the live microbes (most commercial sauerkraut/kimchi in glass jars on shelf-stable sections is pasteurised; refrigerated section is usually live). Yogurt: choose plain, look for 'live and active cultures'. Sugar-free is better but plain unsweetened still beats no fermented foods.
contraindications: Severe immunocompromise. Histamine intolerance. Salt-restricted diet (some choices). Pregnancy: pasteurised choices (commercial yogurt, miso, sauerkraut from glass jars) are safe; raw kombucha and home-fermented foods are typically avoided.
side_effects: Initial bloating/gas (microbiome adjustment, settles in 2-4 weeks). Histamine reactions in susceptible individuals (flushing, headache). Sodium load matters for kimchi/miso/sauerkraut (~500-1000 mg sodium per serving).

name: Finasteride
one_liner: Hair-loss drug with intriguing mortality signal in a large cohort study.
plain_summary: Finasteride is a hair-loss and prostate drug that turned out to have an intriguing mortality signal in a big cohort study. Don't start it just for longevity — the sexual side effects (in 2–5% of men, sometimes persistent) are a real cost. If you're already taking it for hair loss or BPH, the longevity data is at least reassuring.
cost_signal: $10–$30/month generic.
who_should_consider: Men with male-pattern baldness or moderate-to-severe BPH symptoms.
who_should_skip: Anyone considering it solely for longevity. Men trying to conceive (effects on semen possible). Any pregnant woman should avoid handling the drug.
verdict: Don't take this for longevity alone. If you're taking it for hair loss or BPH, the mortality data is reassuring; if you're not, the evidence isn't strong enough to start.
what_to_do: Don't start this purely for longevity. The evidence isn't strong enough.
If you're taking it for hair loss (1 mg/day) or BPH (5 mg/day), the longevity cohort data is reassuring — you're not paying a hidden mortality cost.
Topical finasteride (1% solution applied to scalp) is an alternative for hair loss with much lower systemic exposure and side-effect rate.
Discuss sexual side-effect risk with your doctor before starting. Most men have no issues; a minority have lasting changes.
If you develop sexual side effects, stop the drug — most resolve within months.
Pregnant women must NEVER handle crushed/broken tablets — the drug is teratogenic to male fetuses through skin contact.
If you have liver disease, get cleared by your doctor.
contraindications: Pregnancy (crumbled tablets exposure to women is teratogenic)

name: Fisetin
one_liner: Senolytic flavonoid; clears senescent cells in mice. Human trials underway.
plain_summary: Fisetin is a plant compound (found in strawberries, in trace amounts) that selectively kills off 'zombie' senescent cells in mice — old damaged cells that hang around secreting inflammation. The Mayo Clinic is running human trials right now. It's the most credible 'longevity supplement' with a real mechanism, and the protocol is unusual: high doses for 2 days a month, not daily.
cost_signal: $15–$40/month with monthly pulse dosing.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 willing to bet on the senolytic hypothesis with modest cost and reasonable safety profile. People with osteoarthritis or chronic low-grade inflammation may have biggest theoretical upside.
who_should_skip: Active cancer (theoretical concerns either direction). On anticoagulants. Pregnant.
verdict: Most credible 'longevity supplement' with a real mechanism. Hit-and-run dosing is biologically reasonable. Watch for the Mayo human trial readouts. Until then: low cost, low risk, plausible upside.
what_to_do: Hit-and-run protocol: 1000–1500 mg/day for 2 consecutive days, once per month. Not daily.
Take with fat (olive oil, full-fat yogurt, fatty meal) — bioavailability is poor without it.
Look for 'novusetin' or liposomal fisetin formulations — better absorbed than plain capsules.
Standard caps (100–500 mg/day daily) are the alternative if you don't want pulse dosing — weaker theoretical case but sometimes used.
Stop and reassess if you're on anticoagulants — theoretical interaction.
Watch the Mayo Clinic Phase 2 trial readouts (search 'fisetin senolytic clinical trial' periodically).
Don't combine with active cancer treatment without oncologist sign-off.

name: Flaxseed (ground)
one_liner: Rodriguez-Leyva 2013 RCT: 30g/day reduced SBP by 10 mmHg in hypertensives over 6 months. Bigger BP effect than most foods.
plain_summary: Flaxseed is a workhorse food: high in fibre, the plant omega-3 alpha-linolenic acid (ALA), and lignans (plant compounds with mild estrogen-modulating activity). The most striking result is from Rodriguez-Leyva 2013 — 30g of ground flaxseed daily for 6 months reduced systolic blood pressure by 10 mmHg in patients with peripheral artery disease. That's a bigger effect than most BP foods produce. It also modestly reduces LDL and is one of the few plant sources with measurable cardiovascular outcomes. Grind whole flaxseed before eating (whole seeds pass through undigested); 1-2 tablespoons daily added to oats, yogurt, or smoothies.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated BP, LDL, or constipation. People wanting to add plant omega-3 without fish. Vegetarians/vegans. Postmenopausal women (lignan effect on hot flashes is small but real per Pruthi 2012).
who_should_skip: Anyone with bowel obstruction or severe IBS-C (the fibre load can backfire). Pregnancy (lignan effects on hormones make this conservative; food doses fine, supplement doses unclear). Anticoagulant users at high doses (ALA/lignan platelet effects).
verdict: Boring, cheap, evidence-based. The 6-month BP RCT in PAD patients (10 mmHg SBP reduction) is unusually strong for a food. Add 1-2 Tbsp ground flaxseed to your oats or yogurt daily. Don't substitute flaxseed oil for ground flax — different intervention. Don't expect hot-flash relief; that part of the marketing didn't replicate.
what_to_do: 1-2 tablespoons (10-20g) ground flaxseed daily. Grind whole seeds in a coffee grinder and refrigerate (oxidises within days at room temp). Add to oats, yogurt, smoothies, or baked goods. 30g/day was used in the strongest trial; that's 3 tablespoons. Drink water — fibre needs hydration. Don't substitute flax oil for ground flax — you lose the fibre and lignans.
contraindications: Bowel obstruction. IBS-C with severe fibre intolerance. Pregnancy at supplement doses (food doses fine). Concurrent high-dose anticoagulants (theoretical bleeding risk via ALA + lignan platelet effects).
side_effects: GI bloating in the first 1-2 weeks (gut adaptation). Constipation if not hydrating. Allergy rare but possible.

name: Folate (Vitamin B9)
one_liner: Essential B-vitamin. Mandatory in pregnancy (prevents neural tube defects). Modest stroke-prevention evidence in folate-naive populations. Mostly handled by fortified foods.
plain_summary: Essential B-vitamin. THE one mandatory supplement during pregnancy and pre-pregnancy — it prevents about 70% of neural tube birth defects. For everyone else, fortified flour and leafy greens cover most needs. There's modest stroke-prevention evidence (21% reduction) in populations that don't have folate-fortified food, but the U.S. and most of Europe fortify flour so the marginal benefit of supplements is small.
cost_signal: $3-8/month for generic folic acid 0.4-0.8mg. Methylfolate slightly more.
who_should_consider: Trying to conceive or pregnant — MANDATORY
MTHFR C677T variant (use methylfolate form)
Hypertension in non-folate-fortified country
Heavy alcohol use (impairs folate absorption)
who_should_skip: You eat leafy greens and live in a fortified-flour country (US, Canada) — diet covers it
You're not testing B12 alongside — masking risk
verdict: Mandatory periconceptionally — non-negotiable. Otherwise: covered by fortified flour and leafy greens for most Western diets. If you live in a non-fortified country and have hypertension, low folate intake may be a real lever (per CSPPT). Always test/supplement B12 alongside folate to avoid masking B12 deficiency.
what_to_do: If pregnant or trying: 0.4-0.8 mg folic acid (or methylfolate) daily, starting 1+ months before conception, continuing through 12 weeks gestation. Higher dose (4mg) if prior NTD pregnancy. For everyone else: leafy greens (spinach, romaine), legumes, fortified bread/cereal cover requirements. Supplement only if intake is poor or you live in a non-fortified country with hypertension. Always co-test B12 if supplementing (folate can mask B12-deficiency anemia).
contraindications: High-dose unmasking B12 deficiency without simultaneous B12 testing
side_effects: May mask B12 deficiency (correcting the anemia while neuropathy progresses)

name: Garlic (raw or aged)
one_liner: Modest BP and lipid reductions in 30+ RCTs. Stiebeling 2024 added a 1500-person trial confirming the BP signal.
plain_summary: Garlic has been studied seriously for blood pressure and cholesterol since the 1990s. Multiple meta-analyses of 30+ RCTs find consistent (if modest) reductions in systolic blood pressure (~5-8 mmHg) and total cholesterol. The effect is bigger than most foods produce but smaller than medication. Two forms have evidence: raw garlic at meal-relevant doses (1-2 cloves/day) and aged-garlic-extract supplements (which we have a separate page for). Cooked-garlic effects are weaker because heating destroys some of the active compounds. Add it to food when reasonable; it's not magical but it earns its place.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated BP or cholesterol who want a low-cost food approach. People who already cook with garlic — increase the raw/lightly-cooked portion.
who_should_skip: Pre-surgical patients (raw garlic affects platelet function — stop 7-10 days before surgery). Anyone on warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants (theoretical bleeding risk; clinical reports rare but caution warranted). GERD/reflux. People close to whom you regret your breath afterwards.
verdict: Real, modest, low-cost cardiovascular help from a food. The recent Stiebeling 2024 trial replicated decades of meta-analyses in 1,500 people. Use 1-2 raw cloves daily (crushed and rested 10 minutes before cooking), or aged garlic extract if you want the cardiovascular evidence without the breath cost. Don't expect statin-magnitude effects.
what_to_do: 1-2 raw cloves chopped or crushed daily, OR 600-1500mg aged garlic extract. If using raw garlic for cardiovascular benefit: crush, let sit 10 minutes (enzymatic activation), then add to food at the end of cooking or eat raw. Cooked at high heat from start: still adds flavour but reduces the active compound by 50-90%. Allicin-stabilised supplements (enteric coated with measured allicin yield) are a reasonable middle ground. Take with food to reduce GI upset.
contraindications: Pre-surgery (stop 7-10 days before). Severe GERD. Active bleeding disorder. Pregnancy at supplement doses (food doses fine).
side_effects: GI upset, breath/body odour, occasional heartburn. Bleeding risk with concurrent anticoagulants (clinical reports rare). Allicin-stabilised supplements better tolerated than raw at higher doses.

name: Ginger
one_liner: Strong evidence for nausea (chemotherapy + pregnancy + motion). Mixed but real signal for HbA1c and inflammation markers.
plain_summary: Ginger has the unusual property of being a food with multiple high-quality RCTs showing real clinical effects. The strongest evidence is for nausea — chemotherapy-induced, pregnancy-related, and motion sickness — where it consistently performs as well as some prescription antiemetics. Beyond nausea: meta-analyses show modest reductions in HbA1c (in T2D), CRP (inflammation marker), menstrual pain, and joint pain in osteoarthritis. The food is cheap, low-risk, and has actual clinical utility. Make it part of cooking; consider supplement form for specific conditions.
who_should_consider: Pregnant women with morning sickness (extensive safety data; ACOG-supported). Cancer patients undergoing emetogenic chemo. Adults with T2D wanting a low-risk dietary addition. People with osteoarthritis or chronic inflammation looking for adjunct support. Anyone who likes the taste — easy to incorporate.
who_should_skip: Anticoagulant users (theoretical bleeding risk; reports rare but reasonable caution). People with gallstones (ginger stimulates bile secretion). Severe reflux. Pre-surgery (stop 1 week before, like garlic).
verdict: Quietly useful food. Strong evidence for nausea (pregnancy, chemo, motion sickness) and modest evidence for HbA1c and chronic inflammation. Not a longevity headline, but a solid kitchen-pharmacy item — especially for women with morning sickness (where ACOG endorses it) and T2D patients adding to standard care. Not a replacement for ondansetron in cancer therapy, but a meaningful adjunct.
what_to_do: 1-3g/day of fresh ginger root (a thumb-sized piece adds ~3g) added to cooking, tea, or smoothies. For specific conditions: 500-1000mg standardised ginger extract 2-3x daily for nausea (chemo, pregnancy, motion). For HbA1c/inflammation: 1-2g/day for 8+ weeks. Ginger tea is a reasonable food approach; commercial ginger ales are mostly sugar with negligible ginger.
contraindications: Active anticoagulation (caution, not absolute). Gallstones. Pre-surgery (stop 1 week before). Severe GERD.
side_effects: Heartburn, mild GI upset at higher doses. Theoretical bleeding risk (clinical reports rare). Lower BP modestly (relevant if you're already on antihypertensives — usually beneficial).

name: GLP-1 Agonists (Semaglutide, Tirzepatide)
one_liner: Major weight-loss class with proven cardiovascular mortality reduction in obese patients.
plain_summary: If you're carrying significant excess weight that diet and exercise haven't shifted, this drug class (Wegovy, Zepbound, Mounjaro) is the most powerful tool we've ever had — 15–22% bodyweight loss over a year. Reversing obesity reverses most metabolic disease, which is where most of the longevity benefit comes from. The catch: you also lose some muscle, so you must lift weights and eat enough protein while taking it.
cost_signal: $700–$1,500/month list price. Insurance-covered for some; many compounded versions exist at $200–500/month with varying quality risk.
who_should_consider: Adults with BMI 30+, or 27+ with diabetes/hypertension/dyslipidemia, who've tried lifestyle changes without success.
who_should_skip: Personal/family history of medullary thyroid cancer or MEN-2. Active pancreatitis. Pregnancy. Severe gastroparesis. People with normal weight chasing aesthetic loss without medical indication.
verdict: If you're carrying significant excess weight, this is the most powerful pharmacological tool we've ever had. Pair with resistance training to protect lean mass. The longevity benefit is largely the indirect effect of reversing obesity-driven metabolic disease.
what_to_do: If your BMI is 30+ (or 27+ with metabolic disease), talk to your doctor about semaglutide (Wegovy) or tirzepatide (Zepbound).
Start at the lowest dose and titrate up monthly to manage GI side effects.
Eat enough protein — 1.6 g/kg bodyweight or more. Otherwise ~30% of weight lost will be muscle.
Lift weights 2–3 times a week the entire time you're on it. Non-negotiable for muscle preservation.
Stay hydrated. Many people forget to drink because they aren't hungry.
Watch for severe abdominal pain (pancreatitis), severe vomiting (gastroparesis), or jaundice. Stop and call doctor.
Plan for what happens when you stop. Most people regain most of the weight unless habits have changed during the time on the drug.
contraindications: Personal/family history of medullary thyroid cancer
side_effects: Lean mass loss (~30% of total weight)

name: Glucosamine Sulfate
one_liner: Originally a joint supplement, but two large observational studies (UK Biobank, NHANES) link daily use to ~15% lower all-cause and CV mortality.
plain_summary: Originally a joint supplement, but two huge observational studies (one with half a million people from the UK Biobank) linked daily use to about 15% lower risk of dying from any cause and 22% lower risk of dying from heart disease. Important caveat: these aren't randomized trials, so it could be that people who take glucosamine are also healthier in other ways. But the signal is unusually strong and consistent.
cost_signal: $10-20/month for generic glucosamine sulfate. Branded crystalline form (Dona) is ~$30-50/month.
who_should_consider: You have knee or hip osteoarthritis pain — might help both joints and longevity
You're 60+ and want low-risk hedges
You can't tolerate exercise — this is one of few interventions requiring no effort
who_should_skip: You're healthy and exercising regularly — exercise dwarfs this benefit
You have shellfish allergy (most products are shellfish-derived)
You're prioritizing cost-effectiveness — sauna + omega-3 likely better per dollar
verdict: Interesting observational mortality signal but no RCT-grade evidence. Cheap and safe. Worth considering if you have OA pain — may help joints AND have a tail-risk longevity benefit. Skip if you're healthy and pursuing cost-effectiveness; sauna and omega-3 likely better per dollar.
what_to_do: If you decide to try: 1500 mg glucosamine SULFATE per day (specifically the sulfate form — the cheaper hydrochloride form has weaker evidence). Take with food. The branded 'crystalline glucosamine sulfate' (Rotta, Dona) used in European RCTs is the gold-standard formulation; cheaper alternatives may not deliver the same dose.
contraindications: Shellfish allergy (most products are shellfish-derived; vegetable-source available)
side_effects: Possible glucose intolerance at high doses (small effect)

name: Glycine
one_liner: Cheap amino acid with small but consistent RCT evidence for improving sleep onset and quality.
plain_summary: A cheap amino acid (a building block of protein, like in collagen) that helps you fall asleep faster. Small studies show 3g before bed cuts sleep onset time and improves sleep quality. Effect is modest but real and consistent across studies.
cost_signal: $8-15/month for bulk glycine powder. Magnesium glycinate ~$15/month.
who_should_consider: You take >30 minutes to fall asleep most nights
You wake up feeling unrefreshed
You want a cheaper, safer alternative to melatonin
You already need magnesium — get the glycinate form
who_should_skip: You sleep fine — no upside
You have a serious sleep disorder (apnea, RLS) — fix the underlying issue first
verdict: Cheap, safe, modest sleep benefit. Worth trying if sleep latency is your bottleneck. Magnesium glycinate gives you both at once. Don't expect dramatic results — this is a small effect.
what_to_do: 3 g glycine powder (a rounded teaspoon) dissolved in water, 30-60 minutes before bed. Or take magnesium glycinate (300-400 mg of magnesium gives you ~2g glycine). Slightly sweet taste.
side_effects: Mild GI upset at high doses

name: GlyNAC (Glycine + N-Acetylcysteine)
one_liner: Glutathione-restoring combo with surprisingly strong human pilot data in older adults.
plain_summary: GlyNAC is a simple combination of two amino acids — glycine and N-acetylcysteine — that, in pilot studies in older adults, dramatically restored glutathione levels and improved many aging markers (insulin sensitivity, inflammation, cognitive function, walking speed) in just 16 weeks. The effects are so striking they need replication, but the rationale is solid and the cost is low. Among the more interesting underrated supplements on this site.
cost_signal: $30–$60/month for both amino acids in bulk powder.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 willing to bet on a high-mechanism, lower-evidence intervention. Strongest theoretical case for those with metabolic syndrome features.
who_should_skip: Active peptic ulcer. Asthmatics on certain inhalers (consult MD). Pregnancy (no data).
verdict: If even half the Sekhar effects replicate, GlyNAC is one of the most powerful single interventions on this list. Cheap, generally safe, multiple plausible mechanisms. Take with caution about over-extrapolating from one lab.
what_to_do: Dose used in trials: glycine 100 mg/kg/day + NAC 100 mg/kg/day. For a 70 kg adult: ~7 g glycine + ~7 g NAC daily, split across meals.
Glycine is sweet-tasting and dissolves in water/coffee easily. NAC has a slight sulfur taste.
Start lower (3 g of each) for the first week to gauge GI tolerance, then build up.
Take with food. Split into 2–3 doses per day.
If you have asthma and use a sulfa-containing inhaler, NAC interaction is theoretical but possible — talk to your doctor.
If you have active peptic ulcer disease, hold off until healed — high-dose amino acids can aggravate.
Reasonable to try for 12–16 weeks with before/after bloodwork (CRP, fasting glucose, HbA1c). If meaningful improvements show up, continue.

name: Green Tea (drinking)
one_liner: Multiple Japanese cohort studies link 3+ cups/day to lower CV and dementia mortality. Different from isolated EGCG capsules.
plain_summary: Green tea is one of the few beverages with consistent population-level evidence for both cardiovascular and dementia mortality. Japanese cohort studies (where green tea drinking is common and well-documented) repeatedly show that drinking 3-5 cups/day associates with 20-30% lower cardiovascular mortality and 10-20% lower dementia incidence vs non-drinkers. The evidence is observational, not RCT — but the consistency across multiple large prospective studies puts it ahead of most foods. *This is different from EGCG capsules*, which are concentrated extracts that occasionally cause liver injury at high doses. Drinking the tea is safe and probably worth doing if you like it.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated cardiovascular risk, family history of dementia, or who already drink coffee and want a calmer caffeine source. Worth trying if you don't like coffee.
who_should_skip: Iron-deficiency anaemia (catechins inhibit non-heme iron absorption — drink between meals if iron is a concern). Severe anxiety with caffeine sensitivity. Anyone on warfarin (theoretical vitamin K modulation; data weak). Pregnancy: ≤2 cups/day is safe; very high intake can deplete folate.
verdict: If you like it, drink it. The Japanese cohort data is unusually strong for a beverage. 3-5 cups daily is realistic, low-cost, and likely contributes to the protective effects observed in long-lived Japanese populations. Don't take EGCG isolate capsules instead — they're not the same exposure profile. Brewed tea is safer and arguably more effective.
what_to_do: Drink 3-5 cups of green tea per day, brewed from loose leaf or quality tea bags (not bottled sweetened drinks). Steep 2-3 minutes in hot but not boiling water (~75-80°C; boiling destroys catechins). Matcha gives you the most catechins per serving but costs more. Avoid sweetening — sugar undoes the cardiovascular benefit. *Don't take EGCG isolate capsules* without medical supervision (rare hepatotoxicity at >800mg/day).
contraindications: Pregnancy (limit to 2 cups/day for caffeine). Severe iron-deficiency anaemia (timing matters). Concurrent EGCG isolate capsules (avoid stacking; redundant and hepatotoxic risk).
side_effects: Caffeine: sleep disruption, jitter (mitigated by L-theanine vs coffee). Iron absorption inhibition with meals. At extreme intake (>10 cups/day): nausea, dizziness. Brewed tea is very low risk; concentrated EGCG capsules can cause hepatotoxicity at >800mg/day.

name: Menopausal Hormone Therapy (estrogen ± progesterone)
one_liner: Started within 10 years of menopause, HRT reduces all-cause mortality ~30% and bone fractures substantially. Misunderstood for two decades after the WHI; the timing-hypothesis evidence is now strong.
plain_summary: Menopausal hormone therapy (HRT) was the most-prescribed medication for older women in the 1990s, then almost vanished after the 2002 Women's Health Initiative (WHI) reported small breast-cancer and CV-event signals. The story has since been substantially revised. The WHI enrolled mostly women 10+ years past menopause; subsequent analyses showed HRT *started within 10 years of menopause* (the 'timing hypothesis') has a different risk-benefit profile — including reduction in CV mortality, all-cause mortality, fractures, and possibly cognitive outcomes. The KEEPS, ELITE, and DOPS trials all support this. For most women starting HRT around the time of menopause for vasomotor symptoms, the absolute risks are small and the benefits are real. Decision is individual; the framing has changed.
who_should_consider: Women within 10 years of menopause with vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes, night sweats), genitourinary syndrome, or accelerated bone loss. Women with surgical menopause (especially under 45, where untreated estrogen deficiency causes substantial CV/bone harm). Women at high risk for osteoporotic fracture. Women whose quality of life is impaired by menopausal symptoms.
who_should_skip: Personal history of breast or endometrial cancer (modest absolute risk increase from prolonged use). History of unprovoked VTE (DVT/PE) — relative contraindication for oral; transdermal may still be considered. Active liver disease. Severe uncontrolled hypertension. Pregnancy. >10 years past menopause without prior HRT (timing-hypothesis evidence suggests less benefit, possibly more risk; case-by-case discussion). Active or recent CV disease.
verdict: If you're a woman within 10 years of menopause with vasomotor symptoms or accelerated bone loss, HRT is reasonable to discuss seriously. The 2002 WHI scared a generation of clinicians and patients; the modern evidence is more nuanced. Transdermal formulations preferred when VTE risk matters. Don't start de novo >10 years past menopause without specific indication. The decision is individual; the modern guidelines (NAMS 2022 position statement, ESHRE/ECTS) support selective use.
what_to_do: Discuss with a clinician familiar with the modern evidence — many primary-care providers were trained during the post-WHI period and may default to declining HRT despite updated guidelines. The North American Menopause Society and ESHRE both publish current position statements supporting selective use. Common regimens: transdermal estradiol patch (50-100 mcg/day) or estradiol gel + oral micronized progesterone (200mg/day if intact uterus, ~14 days/cycle for cyclic regimen or daily for continuous). For surgical menopause: estrogen alone (no uterus = no progesterone needed). Bioidentical compounded products are no better-evidenced than FDA-approved formulations and have less consistent dosing. Symptom-guided dose; many women do well on lower doses than originally tested.
contraindications: Personal history of breast/endometrial cancer (relative). Unprovoked VTE history. Active liver disease. Severe uncontrolled HTN. Active CV disease. Pregnancy.
side_effects: Breast tenderness, bloating, breakthrough bleeding (early months), headache, mood changes. VTE risk with oral formulations (~2x baseline; transdermal does not appear to increase VTE per ESTHER and other observational data). Small absolute breast cancer risk increase with combined estrogen+progestin >5 years.

name: Iodine
one_liner: Essential thyroid nutrient. Boring if adequate, important if vegan/low-iodized-salt, risky at kelp-megadose levels.
plain_summary: Iodine is not an anti-aging supplement. It is an essential nutrient for thyroid hormone. Most people get enough from iodized salt, dairy, seafood, or eggs. People avoiding all of those can drift low. The mistake is overcorrecting with kelp capsules that contain wildly variable iodine doses.
cost_signal: $2-8/month if supplementing. Iodized salt is cheaper.
who_should_consider: Vegans, people using only non-iodized specialty salt, low seafood/dairy/egg intake, pregnancy planning with clinician guidance.
who_should_skip: People with thyroid disease should not self-dose high iodine. Avoid kelp megadoses.
verdict: Check the basics: are you using iodized salt or eating iodine-rich foods? If not, a low-dose iodine supplement can make sense. Do not take high-dose kelp for longevity.
what_to_do: Use iodized salt at home if sodium allowance permits.
If supplementing, use about 150 mcg/day, not milligram doses.
Pregnancy requires iodine adequacy but should be handled with prenatal guidance.
Avoid kelp products unless iodine content is verified and moderate.
If you have thyroid disease, ask your clinician before changing iodine.
contraindications: Active thyroid disease without clinician guidance
side_effects: Both deficiency and excess can disrupt thyroid function

name: L-Theanine
one_liner: Amino acid found in green tea. Promotes calm focus without sedation. Modest evidence for stress and sleep quality, no effect on lifespan.
plain_summary: An amino acid in green tea that gives you 'calm focus' — the reason green tea feels different from coffee even though both have caffeine. About 200mg before stressful tasks reduces anxiety without making you sleepy. Cheap and very safe.
cost_signal: $8-15/month for generic L-theanine capsules.
who_should_consider: Coffee gives you jitters or anxiety — pair it with theanine
You have stress-related sleep issues
You want a light, non-prescription anxiolytic
