who_should_skip: You don't drink coffee and aren't stressed — no real upside
You're chasing longevity per se — no mortality data, this is a quality-of-life supplement
verdict: Cheap and safe. Real but small effect on stress/focus. Not a longevity intervention per se — won't extend your life. But might make you nicer to be around when stressed, and that's worth something. Skip if you don't drink coffee or have stress issues.
what_to_do: 200 mg L-theanine taken with morning coffee (the 1:2 theanine:caffeine ratio is studied) for calm focus. Or 200-400 mg an hour before bed for stress/sleep. Available as standalone capsules or paired with caffeine in many nootropic stacks.

name: Legumes (beans, lentils, chickpeas)
one_liner: Strongest single dietary predictor of longevity in Buettner's Blue Zone analyses. Real RCT data for HbA1c and LDL.
plain_summary: If you forced one dietary recommendation onto a longevity site, legumes might be the one. Across the Blue Zones (long-lived populations: Sardinia, Okinawa, Loma Linda, Nicoya, Ikaria), legume consumption is the most consistent dietary pattern. Beans, lentils, chickpeas and the like are cheap, fibre-rich, protein-rich, and affect both blood sugar and cholesterol favourably in real RCTs. Bazzano 2011 and Ha 2014 meta-analyses confirm: 1 cup of cooked legumes daily reduces LDL by about 5-7%, fasting glucose modestly, and HbA1c in T2D. They're the most evidence-supported food intervention you've probably never made a habit of.
cost_signal: Free–$10/mo
who_should_consider: Adults wanting to improve LDL, glycemic control, or weight without medication. People wanting to reduce meat without losing protein. Anyone trying to make Mediterranean/Blue Zone patterns concrete.
who_should_skip: Severe IBS-FODMAP intolerance (legume oligosaccharides can be aggravating; tolerance often improves with gradual introduction and bean-soaking). Acute gout flare (purines in lentils — chronic intake doesn't appear to increase gout risk per Choi 2008, but acute flare avoidance is reasonable). Severe kidney disease at advanced stage (potassium load matters).
verdict: If you make one dietary change after reading this site, make it daily legumes. The Blue Zones are messy ethnographically but the legume signal is unambiguous. Real RCT data for cholesterol and glycemic control. Cheaper than every supplement. Eat 1 cup of cooked beans, lentils, or chickpeas most days — in soup, salad, hummus, bowls, anything. The cumulative effect on cholesterol, glucose, satiety, and gut microbiome is bigger than most pills produce.
what_to_do: 1 cup (240ml) cooked legumes most days of the week. Easy ways: chickpeas in salad/hummus, lentils in soup/stew, black beans in bowls/burritos, white beans mashed on toast. Canned (low sodium) or dried both work. The Mediterranean and Blue Zone consumption pattern is meaningful daily-or-every-other-day, not occasional. Combine with whole grains for complete amino acid profile (the rice-and-beans combo is dietary engineering refined over centuries).
contraindications: Severe IBS-FODMAP intolerance. Active gout flare (chronic intake fine). Stage 4-5 CKD with hyperkalemia. Some gastric bypass populations (high fiber load).
side_effects: Initial gas/bloating from oligosaccharides; tolerance develops over weeks. Cooking dried beans with kombu/bay leaf or soaking overnight reduces this. Iron/zinc/calcium absorption modestly inhibited by phytates (significant only in mixed diets at very high intake).

name: Lion's Mane Mushroom
one_liner: Edible mushroom with two small human RCTs in mild cognitive impairment. Promising but underpowered.
plain_summary: Lion's mane (Hericium erinaceus) is an edible mushroom that contains compounds called erinacines and hericenones. In cell and animal studies, these compounds increase nerve growth factor (NGF) — a protein that helps neurons survive and grow new connections. Two small human trials in older adults with mild cognitive impairment showed cognitive scores improved on lion's mane and declined again after stopping. The trials were small (~30 people each), so the data is suggestive rather than definitive. Worth knowing about; not yet a foundation.
who_should_consider: Adults 50+ with mild cognitive concerns, family history of dementia, or wanting low-risk cognitive support. People who prefer food-derived interventions over isolates.
who_should_skip: Pregnant or nursing (no safety data). Anyone with mushroom allergy. People on anticoagulants (theoretical bleeding risk; no trial confirmation but caution warranted). Don't expect dramatic effects — the trial deltas were modest.
verdict: Suggestive but unproven. Two small human RCTs, strong mechanistic plausibility, low risk profile. If you have cognitive concerns and want a low-risk addition to a diet-and-exercise foundation, it's reasonable. Don't expect dramatic effects — trial deltas were real but modest. Wait for larger replication before treating as foundational.
what_to_do: 1–3 g/day of dual-extract powder (fruiting body + mycelium) or 500–1000 mg of standardized extract. Take with food. Effects in trials emerged after 8–16 weeks of continuous use; trials also showed effect disappeared 4 weeks after stopping. If you try it, plan for at least a 12-week trial.
contraindications: Mushroom allergy. Concurrent anticoagulant therapy (theoretical, not confirmed). No data in pregnancy/lactation.
side_effects: Generally well-tolerated in trials. Rare GI upset. Theoretical immune-modulating effects from beta-glucans (no clinical reports of harm).

name: Lithium (Low-Dose / Microdose)
one_liner: Microdosing lithium has cohort data on suicide and dementia. Don't confuse with psychiatric doses.
plain_summary: Microdose lithium — 1/100th of psychiatric doses — has cohort data linking it to lower suicide rates and lower dementia incidence in regions where drinking water naturally contains it. Taken as a supplement (lithium orotate, 1–5 mg/day) it's well-tolerated. Modest evidence, low cost, low risk — a hedge, not a headline.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 with family history of dementia or depression. Healthy adults wanting a low-cost neuroprotective hedge.
who_should_skip: Pregnancy. Renal impairment. On diuretics or daily NSAIDs. Anyone with bipolar disorder should be on physician-managed therapeutic dosing, not microdose self-management.
verdict: Plausible mild benefit at microdose; safety profile favorable at <5mg/day. Not a headline longevity intervention.
what_to_do: Dose: 1–5 mg of elemental lithium per day, typically as lithium orotate (the orotate carrier itself isn't important — lithium is doing the work).
Lithium orotate 5 mg = ~0.5 mg elemental lithium. Read the label carefully; products vary.
Take in the morning with food. No need for blood monitoring at microdose levels (unlike psychiatric doses).
If you have any kidney impairment or are on diuretics: skip this entirely. Lithium concentrates in kidneys and interactions are unforgiving.
Pregnancy: skip.
If you ever feel tremor, increased thirst, or confusion: stop and check thyroid + kidney function. These are signs of too-high lithium.
Don't combine with high-dose ibuprofen or other NSAIDs daily — they raise lithium levels.

name: Low / No Alcohol
one_liner: The 'small amount is good' story is dead. Less is consistently better.
plain_summary: The 'a glass of red wine is good for you' story is dead. Updated analyses controlling for confounders show every additional drink raises cancer risk and disrupts sleep. Less is consistently better. If you drink heavily, cutting back is one of the highest-leverage things on this site. If you drink moderately, you're not getting health benefits from continuing — but social ritual is real, so consider lower-alcohol or non-alcoholic alternatives.
cost_signal: Net savings. Reducing alcohol intake almost always saves money.
who_should_consider: Anyone who drinks more than 1–2 standard drinks most days. Anyone with poor sleep, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, or family history of breast/GI cancers.
who_should_skip: If you don't drink, don't start. People in recovery — protect that fully.
verdict: If you drink heavily, cutting back is high-leverage. If you drink moderately, the modern evidence does not support continuing for health. Sleep alone improves measurably. Social ritual is real, but consider lower-alcohol or non-alcoholic options.
what_to_do: If you currently drink more than 7 (women) or 14 (men) drinks/week, cut back. Halving is a great start.
Set drink-free days. Aim for at least 3 alcohol-free days per week.
Track honestly for a month — most people underestimate by 30–50%.
Replace with sparkling water, NA beer (most are now genuinely good), kombucha, or tea in social settings.
If you crave it daily, can't stop after 1–2, or feel withdrawal symptoms when you skip — talk to your doctor. This is treatable; willpower alone isn't usually enough.
Best sleep night is a 0-drink night. Notice how you feel in the morning after a sober evening.
Pregnancy: zero alcohol.
side_effects: Sleep disruption even at moderate intake

name: Low-Dose Aspirin
one_liner: Useful for secondary prevention. Net benefit in primary prevention is now disputed.
plain_summary: If you've already had a heart attack or stroke, low-dose aspirin reduces the chance of another one and is usually worth the bleeding risk. If you haven't — and most people haven't — the modern evidence no longer supports taking it 'just in case.' The bleeding risk often eats the cardiovascular benefit. Don't start daily aspirin without a real reason.
cost_signal: $2–$8/month.
who_should_consider: People with established cardiovascular disease (post-MI, post-stroke, stents, established peripheral artery disease) under physician guidance.
who_should_skip: Most healthy adults without prior cardiovascular events. Active GI ulcer or recent bleeding. Severe liver disease. Children with viral illness.
verdict: If you've had an event, take it as your cardiologist directs. If you haven't and you're otherwise low-risk, the modern evidence does not support taking it just for longevity.
what_to_do: If your cardiologist already has you on it after a heart attack, stent, or stroke: keep taking it. Don't stop without checking with them.
If you don't have established heart disease: don't start it 'for prevention' on your own. The 2018–2022 trials (ARRIVE, ASPREE, ASCEND) shifted the consensus.
If you're considering it: discuss with your doctor based on personal cardiovascular risk score AND bleeding risk score. There are calculators they can use.
If on it: take 75–100 mg enteric-coated daily, with food. Avoid alcohol and NSAIDs (extra GI bleeding).
Stop 5–7 days before any surgery (per surgeon's instructions).
Don't give to children or teens with viral illness — Reye's syndrome risk.

name: Lutein + Zeaxanthin
one_liner: Carotenoids that concentrate in the eye's macula. Strong RCT evidence for slowing age-related macular degeneration progression.
plain_summary: Two pigments your eyes use as built-in sunglasses. Concentrated in the part of your retina that lets you read. The biggest eye-supplement study ever (AREDS2) showed they slow down age-related macular degeneration — the leading cause of vision loss after 65.
cost_signal: $15-25/month for AREDS2 multivitamin. Standalone lutein/zeaxanthin similar. Cheaper if you eat ~1 cup spinach/kale daily.
who_should_consider: You're 60+ with family history of AMD
You've been told you have early or intermediate AMD
You don't eat leafy greens regularly
You spend hours daily on screens (theoretical, not proven)
who_should_skip: You're under 40 with no AMD risk and eat greens — diet covers it
You smoke — get the AREDS2 (no beta-carotene) version, but stopping smoking matters more
verdict: Strong evidence for slowing AMD progression in those at risk. If you have AMD risk (family history, age 60+, or already diagnosed with intermediate AMD), this is one of the few supplements with regulatory-grade evidence. For everyone else, eat leafy greens (kale, spinach, egg yolks have it).
what_to_do: Take an AREDS2-formula multivitamin (PreserVision AREDS2, Ocuvite Eye Performance) which contains lutein 10 mg + zeaxanthin 2 mg. One per day with food (these are fat-soluble). Or eat leafy greens daily — kale and spinach are dense sources, and egg yolks have particularly bioavailable lutein.
side_effects: Mild yellow-orange skin discoloration at very high doses (>20 mg lutein/day)

name: Magnesium
one_liner: Common deficiency. Replete it. Repleted state is associated with lower mortality.
plain_summary: Most modern diets quietly run low on magnesium because we eat fewer leafy greens, nuts, and whole grains than our grandparents did. Repleting it is cheap, broadly useful, and worth doing if you don't eat those foods regularly. People with adequate magnesium have lower mortality in cohort studies.
cost_signal: $5–$15/month. Cheapest insurance on this site.
who_should_consider: Most adults eating a Western diet. Anyone with muscle cramps, restless sleep, anxiety, constipation, or migraines (all can have magnesium-deficiency components).
who_should_skip: People already eating lots of leafy greens, nuts, and legumes daily. Severe renal impairment without medical supervision.
verdict: If you don't eat lots of leafy greens, nuts, and beans, supplement. Glycinate or citrate at 200-400mg. Cheap insurance with broad biological plausibility.
what_to_do: First try food: leafy greens, almonds, pumpkin seeds, black beans, dark chocolate, whole grains. If you eat these daily, you may not need to supplement.
If supplementing: 200–400 mg of elemental magnesium per day. Take with food.
Pick a chelated form: glycinate (gentle, calming, good for sleep) or malate (good for daytime). Avoid oxide — poorly absorbed, just causes diarrhea.
Citrate is fine but has a laxative effect at higher doses; some people use it deliberately for that.
Take it in the evening if you find it helps sleep. Anytime works otherwise.
Start with 200 mg; if you get loose stools, halve the dose or switch form.
If you have severe kidney disease or heart block, talk to your doctor first.

name: Meditation / Stress Reduction
one_liner: Modest effects on cardiovascular and inflammatory markers; subjective wellbeing real.
plain_summary: Meditation is real but only if you actually do it — daily, for years. The 'I tried it for a week' dose isn't enough. Sustained practice reliably lowers stress hormones, blood pressure, and inflammation markers, and is associated with slower cellular aging. Free, scalable, no downside (for most people). The hard part is showing up daily.
cost_signal: $0–$15/month for an app. Retreats $300–$2000. The actual practice is free.
who_should_consider: Anyone with chronic stress, hypertension (low-end help), insomnia, anxiety, or who simply wants to age better. Especially valuable in midlife and beyond.
who_should_skip: Active psychosis or severe untreated PTSD without professional support. Don't dive into deep practice during acute crisis.
verdict: Real if you actually practice. The 'I tried it for a week' dose isn't enough. Daily 10-20 min for years gets the meaningful effects. Free, scalable, low downside.
what_to_do: Start with 10 minutes a day. Same time, same place. Boring is fine — boring works.
Use a guided app for the first 1–3 months: Waking Up (Sam Harris), Headspace, or Insight Timer. Calm is more sleep-focused.
Don't try to 'stop your thoughts.' Just notice them and return to your anchor (breath, sound, body sensation).
Build to 20–30 min/day over 6–12 months. The benefits scale with cumulative practice.
Try a 1-day silent retreat after 6 months of daily practice. A multi-day retreat after a year. These compress months of progress into days.
If meditation triggers severe anxiety, dissociation, or worsens trauma symptoms, stop and seek a trauma-informed teacher.
Walking meditation, tai chi, and mindful yoga are valid alternatives if seated meditation doesn't fit you.
side_effects: Rare: meditation-induced anxiety/dissociation in vulnerable individuals

name: Mediterranean Diet
one_liner: Strongest single dietary pattern for cardiovascular and all-cause mortality reduction.
plain_summary: If you only change one thing about how you eat, this is it. Olive oil instead of butter, fish twice a week, vegetables and legumes as the bulk of meals, nuts as snacks, very little red or processed meat. The evidence for living longer on this pattern is unmatched by any single supplement or drug.
cost_signal: Net neutral to slightly more expensive than a Western diet. Good EVOO and fish are the budget pressure points; legumes and seasonal vegetables offset.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone. Strongest evidence base of any diet for longevity.
who_should_skip: People with severe seafood/nut allergies need substitutions. Some kidney conditions limit legume protein. Otherwise universally applicable.
verdict: If you change one thing about your diet, this is it. The evidence is unmatched. Treat olive oil, fish, vegetables, legumes as defaults, not exceptions.
what_to_do: Make extra-virgin olive oil your default cooking and dressing fat. 4+ tablespoons per day is what trial subjects used.
Eat fish twice a week minimum. Sardines, salmon, mackerel, anchovies — the fattier the better.
Legumes (beans, lentils, chickpeas) at least 3 times a week.
Nuts daily — a small handful. Walnuts, almonds, pistachios.
Vegetables at most meals. Diversity matters more than amount.
Whole grains over refined. Berries and fruit as your default sweet.
Red meat once a week or less. Processed meat (sausages, deli, bacon) almost never.
Wine, if you drink, with meals and modest (1 glass for women, 1–2 for men). Skipping is also fine.

name: Melatonin
one_liner: Sleep regulator with broader antioxidant + mitochondrial effects. Lower doses better than commonly sold.
plain_summary: Melatonin isn't just a sleep aid — it's a master signal for your body's daily rhythm and a surprisingly potent antioxidant. Most over-the-counter doses (3–10 mg) are 10–30x higher than what works. Try 0.3–0.5 mg first; bigger doses can cause grogginess and aren't more effective. Cheap, generally safe, useful for jet lag and shift work.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 with declining sleep quality. Travelers crossing time zones. Shift workers. Anyone whose 'sleep onset' is the main problem (not maintenance).
who_should_skip: Pregnant or trying to conceive. On warfarin. Severe autoimmune disease (talk to doctor first). Children unless prescribed.
verdict: Cheap, generally safe, possibly beneficial beyond sleep. Use the lowest effective dose (often 0.3-0.5mg) — bigger isn't better.
what_to_do: Start low: 0.3–0.5 mg, 30–60 min before target bedtime. Most people don't need more.
Buy it where you can get a low dose. Most US bottles are 3–10 mg — break them or buy 0.3 mg specifically (Life Extension and a few others stock low doses).
Take it the same time each night to anchor your rhythm.
For jet lag: 0.5 mg at the new bedtime for 3–5 nights starting the night you arrive (not the night before).
If you wake groggy, your dose is too high.
Skip if you're on warfarin (interaction). Caution with autoimmune conditions (theoretical immune-modulation).
Don't give to kids without a pediatrician — long-term effects on developing endocrine system are unstudied.
side_effects: Hormonal effects at very high doses (theoretical)

name: Metformin
one_liner: Diabetes drug with intriguing longevity signal in observational data. TAME trial in progress.
plain_summary: Metformin is a cheap, decades-old diabetes drug that diabetics on it seem to live longer than even non-diabetics — which is strange and interesting. Whether healthy people benefit is genuinely unclear. The big trial (TAME) is still running. Important catch: it blunts the muscle and fitness gains you get from hard exercise, so if you train seriously, this is a real tradeoff.
cost_signal: $5–$20/month generic. Among the cheapest things on this list.
who_should_consider: People with pre-diabetes, fatty liver, PCOS, or strong family history of type 2 diabetes who don't train at high intensity for performance gains.
who_should_skip: Serious athletes or anyone whose primary goal is maximizing strength/hypertrophy from training. Also: kidney impairment, on contrast dye, or chronic alcohol use.
verdict: Type 2 diabetes: yes, first-line, the data is overwhelming. Non-diabetics for longevity: not yet — the only RCT data shows it BLUNTS exercise gains. If you exercise hard for fitness, metformin is a poor fit. Wait for TAME. If you're pre-diabetic and exercise is hard to maintain, the diabetes-prevention case is stronger.
what_to_do: If you have type 2 diabetes or pre-diabetes — this is probably already on your doctor's table. Talk to them.
If you're healthy and want it for longevity: ask about it explicitly. Most primary docs won't prescribe off-label; longevity-focused clinics will.
Start at 500 mg extended-release with dinner to minimize GI upset. Many people work up to 1000–1500 mg/day over weeks.
If you exercise hard for performance/muscle: take it on rest days only, or skip it on heavy training days. Or skip the drug entirely.
Get a B12 level checked yearly — long-term metformin can quietly drop B12.
Drink water. Stop if you're sick with vomiting/diarrhea (rare risk of lactic acidosis when dehydrated).

name: N-Acetyl Cysteine (NAC)
one_liner: Glutathione precursor with strong evidence for COPD exacerbations and acetaminophen overdose. Modest evidence for general antioxidant benefit.
plain_summary: An amino acid that your body uses to make glutathione, which is your body's most important antioxidant. Best evidence: it cuts COPD flare-ups by about 25-30% in people with chronic lung disease. Originally developed (and still used) as an emergency antidote for Tylenol overdose. For healthy people, the longevity evidence is weaker — combined with glycine (see GlyNAC) shows more promise.
cost_signal: $8-15/month for generic NAC 600mg capsules.
who_should_consider: Diagnosed COPD or chronic bronchitis with frequent exacerbations
You want a glutathione precursor and are 60+
PCOS (modest evidence for menstrual regulation)
Heavy acetaminophen user (theoretical liver protection)
who_should_skip: You're healthy with no respiratory issues — modest benefit
You're already on inhaled mucolytics
You have asthma — discuss with doctor (rare bronchospasm risk)
verdict: Solid evidence for COPD/chronic bronchitis. Plausible but unproven for general anti-aging via glutathione. Cheap, safe at studied doses. Worth considering if you have chronic respiratory issues; less clear value for healthy people. The newer GlyNAC research (with glycine) shows more promise — see GlyNAC entry.
what_to_do: Standard adult dose: 600 mg twice daily with food, or 1200 mg once daily. For COPD: 600 mg twice daily long-term. Take with food to minimize GI upset. The unpleasant sulfur smell is normal — choose enteric-coated capsules if odor bothers you.
contraindications: Asthma (rare bronchospasm with inhaled form; oral generally safe)

name: NMN (Nicotinamide Mononucleotide)
one_liner: Popular NAD+ precursor. Raises NAD+ levels in humans; longevity outcome unproven.
plain_summary: NMN is the trendy 'NAD+ booster' supplement. It does raise NAD+ levels in your blood — that part is real. Whether higher NAD+ actually makes you live longer is genuinely unknown in humans. Sinclair takes it daily; the rigorous trials haven't read out yet. Modest hedge with biological rationale, not magic.
cost_signal: $30–$80/month. Among the more expensive supplements without proven payoff.
who_should_consider: People over 50 who are already doing the basics (sleep, exercise, diet) and want to layer a hopeful but unproven hedge.
who_should_skip: Active cancer patients. Pregnant or breastfeeding (no data). People hoping it'll substitute for lifestyle work.
verdict: If you want to bet on NAD+ restoration, NMN raises the level. Whether that translates into longer life is genuinely unknown. Modest hedge; don't expect dramatic effects.
what_to_do: Dose: 250–1000 mg/day. Most people start at 500 mg.
Oral works (sublingual claims are mostly marketing). Take in the morning — it's mildly stimulating for some.
Brand matters: NAD+ precursor supplements are loosely regulated. Look for third-party tested brands; cheap unbranded versions may have low actual content.
Don't take if you have active cancer — NAD+ supports cell metabolism and the safety case isn't proven for cancer patients.
Don't expect dramatic effects. Sleep, exercise, fiber intake, and not smoking all do far more.
If you take NR or NMN, you don't need both. Pick one.
contraindications: Active cancer (theoretical: NAD+ supports cancer cell metabolism)

name: No Smoking
one_liner: The single largest modifiable mortality risk. Quitting at any age extends life.
plain_summary: If you smoke, quitting is the single highest-impact thing you can possibly do for your health and longevity. Nothing else on this site comes close — not exercise, not diet, not any drug. Quitting at any age extends life; quitting before 40 erases nearly all the excess mortality risk. Use whatever works: nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion, hypnosis, support groups. Just quit.
cost_signal: Quit-aid medications: $50–$200/month for 3–6 months. Replaces cigarettes which cost $200–$500/month — net massive savings.
who_should_consider: Every single person who smokes. Full stop.
who_should_skip: Nobody.
verdict: If you smoke, quitting is the highest-impact thing you can do for longevity, by an enormous margin. There is no supplement or drug on this site that comes close. Use NRT, varenicline, support; whatever works.
what_to_do: Pick a quit date in the next 2 weeks. Tell people. Make it real.
Use medication. Quit success doubles with help. Best options: varenicline (Chantix) or combined nicotine patch + lozenge/gum.
Bupropion (Wellbutrin) is an alternative if varenicline isn't tolerated.
Most people relapse 3–7 times before successful quit. That's normal. Keep trying.
Avoid your old triggers (specific places, drinks, social settings) for the first 1–3 months.
If you vape: that's better than smoking but not zero risk. Plan to quit vaping too once cigarette-free for 6+ months.
Free help: smokefree.gov, your country's quitline, local nicotine support groups.

name: NR (Nicotinamide Riboside)
one_liner: Older sibling of NMN. Same NAD+-raising effect, slightly different pharmacology.
plain_summary: NR is the older sibling of NMN — same general goal of raising NAD+, slightly different pathway, and slightly more human research published. If you're going to bet on NAD+ restoration, NR has marginally better evidence. Same caveat: it raises the level, but whether that adds years to life is unproven.
cost_signal: $40–$80/month for branded Niagen. Generic NR cheaper but less verified.
who_should_consider: Same profile as NMN. Slight preference if you want the better-documented option.
who_should_skip: Active cancer. Pregnancy.
verdict: Same general bet as NMN with more human data and clearer regulatory status. If you want NAD+ supplementation, NR has the slightly better evidence base. Don't expect miracles.
what_to_do: Dose: 300–1000 mg/day. Most clinical trials use 300–600 mg.
Brand: 'Niagen' (ChromaDex) is the patented form with the most published research. Generic NR exists; quality varies more.
Take in the morning with food.
If you flush mildly (warm/red face), it's a known minor effect, usually fades.
Don't combine with NMN — same target, no additive benefit, double the cost.
Skip if you have active cancer (theoretical concern, no proof either way).

name: Oat β-Glucan (Oatmeal, Oat Bran)
one_liner: The cholesterol-lowering soluble fiber in oats. FDA-recognized health claim. Eat oatmeal — about as evidence-based as supplements get.
plain_summary: The fiber in oats (specifically beta-glucan) lowers LDL cholesterol by about 7% when you eat 3+ grams a day. That's roughly one cup of cooked oatmeal. The FDA officially approves the heart-disease-prevention claim — one of very few foods that does. It also slightly smooths blood-sugar spikes after meals. So your grandmother was right about oatmeal.
cost_signal: $3-8/month for rolled or steel-cut oats. Branded oat-fiber supplements ($30-50/mo) are unnecessary if you can eat oats.
who_should_consider: Elevated LDL not yet on a statin
On a statin but want one more lever
Type 2 diabetes — helps post-prandial glucose
You eat breakfast anyway — this is one of the easiest substitutions
who_should_skip: You already eat oatmeal regularly — keep going
Celiac (use certified gluten-free oats)
You hate oatmeal — psyllium is the supplement-form alternative
verdict: Eat oatmeal. The evidence is real, regulatory-grade, and free if you're already eating breakfast. One cup cooked oatmeal hits the 3g β-glucan threshold for the FDA claim. Don't bother with fancy oat-based 'cholesterol-lowering' supplements at 5x the cost. Steel-cut > rolled > instant for slowest glucose response, but rolled oats are still effective.
what_to_do: Eat 1 cup cooked oatmeal, ½ cup raw rolled oats, or 30g oat bran most days. That delivers ≥3g β-glucan, the FDA threshold. Steel-cut oats give the slowest glucose response (highest viscosity). Add berries, nuts, cinnamon — don't drown it in honey. If you can't eat oats: 30g oat bran sprinkled on yogurt or a smoothie also works. Consistency over months matters more than perfection.
contraindications: Celiac disease (cross-contamination risk; use certified gluten-free oats)

name: Extra-Virgin Olive Oil (Polyphenols)
one_liner: The mortality data on EVOO consumption is among the strongest for any 'food'.
plain_summary: Extra-virgin olive oil isn't just a healthy fat — it's one of the most evidence-graded foods on the planet, with mortality reduction in multiple large trials. The polyphenol content matters: cheap supermarket EVOO is far weaker than fresh, dark-bottle, recently-harvested oil. Replace your butter, margarine, and seed oils with high-polyphenol EVOO. This is closer to a 'longevity food' than almost anything else.
cost_signal: Quality EVOO: $20–$50/liter. A liter lasts 1–2 months at recommended intake.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone. Among the highest evidence-graded foods on the planet.
who_should_skip: Olive oil allergy (rare). People needing to dramatically restrict total fat for medical reasons.
verdict: Among the strongest evidence-graded foods on the planet. Replace seed oils and butter with high-polyphenol EVOO. The polyphenol content matters: harvest date, freshness, dark glass bottle. Closest thing to a 'longevity food' that has actual mortality data.
what_to_do: Use 30–50 ml/day (~2–3 tablespoons) as your default cooking and dressing fat.
Buy high-polyphenol EVOO. Look for: harvest date within 12 months, dark glass or tin (NOT clear plastic), 'extra virgin' (not just 'virgin' or 'olive oil'), single-origin if possible.
Better signals of quality: pungent peppery sting at the back of the throat, bitter green flavor. That's polyphenols. Smooth/buttery EVOO has fewer of them.
Store in a dark cupboard, away from heat. Cap tight. Use within 12 months of opening.
Cooking: yes, you can cook with EVOO — its smoke point is high enough for most home use. Don't deep fry.
Drizzle raw on salads, vegetables, soups, eggs, fish, bread. The polyphenols are heat-degraded, so raw use preserves the most.
Brands worth knowing: Brightland (US), Kosterina, Apollo, or local producers if you live in a Mediterranean region.

name: Omega-3 (EPA/DHA)
one_liner: Cardiovascular benefit at high pharmacological doses (icosapent ethyl). Mixed at supplement doses.
plain_summary: Eating fatty fish twice a week probably helps your heart and brain. Taking a basic 1-gram fish oil pill from the pharmacy is mostly waste-of-money for healthy people — the doses tested in real studies are 4–10x higher. If you have high triglycerides or heart disease, prescription-grade EPA at 4 grams is a different story and genuinely works.
cost_signal: Quality fish oil: $15–$40/month. Prescription icosapent ethyl: $300+ without insurance.
who_should_consider: People who don't eat fatty fish twice a week, anyone with elevated triglycerides, history of heart disease, or autoimmune/inflammatory conditions.
who_should_skip: Heavy fatty-fish eaters already getting 2g+ EPA/DHA from food. People on warfarin should consult their doctor.
verdict: If you have elevated triglycerides or established cardiovascular disease, prescription EPA at 4g works. For healthy people taking 1g fish oil for general health: probably doing little. Eat fatty fish 2x/week instead.
what_to_do: First choice: eat fatty fish twice a week — wild salmon, sardines, mackerel, herring, anchovies. This beats supplements for most people.
If you supplement: aim for 2g/day combined EPA+DHA, not the 300mg in cheap pills. Read the label — total grams of fish oil ≠ grams of EPA+DHA.
If you have high triglycerides or established heart disease: ask your doctor about prescription icosapent ethyl (Vascepa) at 4g/day. This has real RCT evidence.
Take with a meal that has fat (improves absorption). Refrigerate to slow oxidation.
Smell test: if it smells fishy/rancid, throw it out. Oxidized fish oil may be worse than no fish oil.
If you're on blood thinners, talk to your doctor before high doses.
side_effects: Mild bleeding tendency at high doses
Atrial fibrillation signal at very high doses (debated)

name: Oral Health
one_liner: Periodontitis is independently associated with cardiovascular and Alzheimer's risk.
plain_summary: Gum disease is one of the most underrated drivers of long-term health damage. Chronically inflamed gums seed bacteria into your bloodstream that show up in heart-artery plaques and Alzheimer's brains. Floss every day, get cleanings twice a year, and treat gum disease aggressively if you have it. This is a longevity lever masquerading as a hygiene chore.
cost_signal: Electric toothbrush $30–$200 one-time. Floss $20/year. Cleanings $80–$200 each, twice a year. Treating gum disease can run $1000–$3000+ but stops the damage.
who_should_consider: Everyone. Especially anyone with cardiovascular risk factors, family history of dementia, or diabetes (bidirectional relationship).
who_should_skip: Nobody.
verdict: Floss. Get cleanings. Treat gum disease aggressively. Underrated longevity lever masquerading as a hygiene chore.
what_to_do: Brush twice a day for 2 full minutes. Electric toothbrush (Oral-B or Sonicare) is meaningfully better than manual.
Floss every night. Not 'when food is stuck' — every night. Water flossers (Waterpik) work for those who hate string floss.
Professional cleaning every 6 months. Every 3–4 months if you have any history of gum disease.
If your dentist mentions 'pockets' or 'periodontitis': take it seriously. Get a deep cleaning (scaling and root planing). May need a periodontist.
Notice bleeding gums when brushing or flossing? That's not normal. It means inflammation. Floss MORE, not less — should improve in 1–2 weeks. If it doesn't, see a dentist.
Tongue scraping in the morning reduces bacterial load.
Avoid antibacterial mouthwash daily — it disrupts the helpful oral microbiome too. Use only short-term if directed.

name: Plant Sterols / Stanols
one_liner: Reliably lowers LDL by about 7-10%. Useful as an ApoB add-on, but not a substitute for statins when risk is high.
plain_summary: Plant sterols and stanols compete with cholesterol absorption in the gut. At the studied dose, they reliably lower LDL. The limitation: we have LDL endpoint evidence, not definitive hard-outcome evidence, and they do nothing for triglycerides, blood pressure, sleep, or muscle.
cost_signal: $10-40/month depending on fortified foods or capsules.
who_should_consider: People with mildly elevated LDL/ApoB who want a food/supplement add-on, especially alongside soluble fiber.
who_should_skip: Sitosterolemia. High ASCVD-risk people who need medication should not use this instead of statins/ezetimibe.
verdict: A narrow but real LDL tool. Think of it like psyllium’s cousin: useful if the target is LDL/ApoB, underwhelming if you expect whole-body longevity magic.
what_to_do: Dose: 1.5-2 g/day plant sterols/stanols with meals.
Use for 8-12 weeks, then recheck LDL-C or ApoB.
Combine with soluble fiber and saturated-fat replacement for additive effect.
Do not use as a substitute for indicated lipid-lowering medication.
side_effects: May slightly reduce carotenoid absorption; eat colorful vegetables

name: Pomegranate (juice or fruit)
one_liner: Real human RCT data for blood pressure and carotid plaque. Strongest food-source polyphenol evidence outside olive oil.
plain_summary: Pomegranate juice or fruit has been tested in real humans for cardiovascular endpoints. Aviram's 2004 trial showed daily pomegranate juice reduced carotid intima-media thickness over 3 years in adults with carotid stenosis — a hard structural marker, not just a biomarker. Multiple smaller trials show modest blood pressure reductions. The polyphenols (punicalagins, ellagic acid) are precursors to urolithin-A, which gets its own page. If you like pomegranate, it earns its place at the table; if you don't, urolithin-A or olive oil cover similar ground.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated blood pressure or known atherosclerosis. People preferring food over supplements for cardiovascular support. Anyone already drinking sweetened beverages (replace one with pomegranate).
who_should_skip: Diabetics or pre-diabetics (the juice has 30g sugar per cup — calories matter). People on ACE inhibitors at maximum dose (additive BP-lowering, low risk but worth knowing). Anyone with severe pollen-fruit syndrome reactive to pomegranate.
verdict: Real food, real cardiovascular data. The 3-year carotid-IMT trial is unusually strong for a food. If you have elevated BP or atherosclerosis and like pomegranate, drink a cup a day — it earns its place. If you don't like it or are watching sugar, urolithin-A capsules cover the metabolite-pathway endpoint, and olive oil + Mediterranean diet covers the broader food-polyphenol case.
what_to_do: Drink 240ml (1 cup) pomegranate juice daily, or eat the fruit equivalent (one medium pomegranate ≈ ½ cup of arils). Choose 100% juice with no added sugar. Best with a meal. If you don't like pomegranate or struggle with sugar in juice, pomegranate-extract capsules (500mg standardized to 40% punicalagins) are a reasonable substitute, though extract trials are smaller than juice trials.
contraindications: Diabetes (juice form — sugar load). Severe pomegranate allergy. Theoretical interaction with statins via CYP3A4 inhibition (smaller effect than grapefruit; clinically minor at typical doses).
side_effects: Generally well-tolerated. Sugar content matters for diabetics. Possible additive BP-lowering with antihypertensives. Rare allergic reactions in pollen-fruit syndrome.

name: Probiotics (Specific Strain Matters)
one_liner: Effect is strain-specific. Strong evidence for antibiotic-associated diarrhea and a few specific GI conditions. Marketing claims about general 'gut health' are mostly hype.
plain_summary: The effect of a probiotic depends entirely on which strain you take and what you're taking it for. Strong evidence: probiotics with Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG or Saccharomyces boulardii cut antibiotic diarrhea roughly in half. Specific strains help specific types of IBS. But the broad consumer marketing — 'gut health,' 'immunity,' 'mood' — is mostly not supported by good trials. Eat yogurt and fermented foods; reach for specific probiotic strains for specific medical reasons.
cost_signal: $15-30/month for evidence-based single-strain products (Culturelle, Florastor). $40-80/month for multi-strain formulations of unclear benefit.
who_should_consider: Currently on antibiotics (the strongest evidence)
Just had C. difficile or are at risk of recurrence
Specific IBS subtype with matching evidence-based strain
Traveler's diarrhea prevention
who_should_skip: Severely immunocompromised (rare bacteremia risk)
Central venous catheter (avoid Saccharomyces specifically)
ICU/critically ill patients
You expect general 'gut health' or longevity benefit — not supported
verdict: Match strain to indication. Buy probiotics for specific reasons (antibiotic course, IBS, traveler's diarrhea), not 'general gut health'. The evidence-based picks: L. rhamnosus GG (Culturelle) or Saccharomyces boulardii (Florastor) when on antibiotics. For IBS, look for studies of the specific strain in your formulation. Skip the multi-strain $50 'gut microbiome' supplements — most are unsupported by their own marketed claims. Fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut) are a cheaper, more pleasant alternative.
what_to_do: If starting an antibiotic: Lactobacillus rhamnosus GG (Culturelle) 10+ billion CFU/day OR Saccharomyces boulardii (Florastor) 250-500 mg twice daily, starting day 1 of antibiotic, continuing 1 week after. For IBS-D: Lactobacillus plantarum 299v specifically. For general gut health: eat yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi — cheaper and more enjoyable. Avoid expensive multi-strain 'gut health' formulations without clinical-trial backing of the specific product.
contraindications: Severe immunocompromise (chemotherapy, HIV with low CD4, organ transplant on heavy immunosuppression)
Critical illness in ICU (avoid Saccharomyces in central-line patients)
side_effects: Rare bacteremia in immunocompromised (avoid in those populations)

name: Higher Protein Intake (1.2-1.6 g/kg)
one_liner: Protein needs rise with age, training, weight loss, and menopause. 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day is the practical longevity range for most adults over 50.
plain_summary: Protein is not a bodybuilding side quest. It is the raw material for muscle, bone, immune proteins, enzymes, and recovery. With age, muscle becomes less sensitive to small protein doses, so the old RDA of 0.8 g/kg/day often under-shoots for preserving strength and independence. The practical target changes by age, sex, training, weight loss, and kidney function.
cost_signal: Mostly food costs. Whey protein supplement: $20-40/month for 30g/day. Plant protein blends similar.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50; postmenopausal women; anyone doing resistance training; people losing weight; vegetarians/vegans with low protein density; adults with low appetite, frailty risk, or recent illness/injury.
who_should_skip: Advanced chronic kidney disease not on dialysis, inherited protein-handling disorders, or clinician-prescribed protein restriction. People already eating enough protein do not need powders just to chase a number.
verdict: Most longevity nutrition advice underplays protein. Aim for enough protein to preserve muscle while keeping the rest of the diet high-fiber and cardiometabolically sane. For many adults over 50, that means 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day, split across meals, paired with resistance training.
what_to_do: Under 50 and sedentary: 0.8-1.2 g/kg/day is usually enough if body composition and appetite are stable.
Training or trying to gain muscle: 1.4-2.0 g/kg/day is the evidence-based sports nutrition range.
Over 50: use 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day as the practical target, especially if strength, appetite, or weight is changing.
During weight loss: lean toward 1.6 g/kg/day, sometimes higher if already lean and resistance training, to preserve lean mass.
Per meal: aim for 25-40 g protein, or about 0.4 g/kg/meal, across 3-4 meals. Breakfast is the meal most people under-dose.
Women: protein matters more after menopause because estrogen loss accelerates changes in muscle, bone, and body composition. Pair protein with heavy resistance training, not dieting alone.
Men: absolute grams are often higher because body mass is higher, but the per-kg target is similar. Do not use protein as an excuse for low fiber or high saturated fat.
Plant-based diets: totally workable, but plan density. Soy foods, legumes, seitan, tofu/tempeh, pea/rice blends, and higher total grams help overcome lower leucine density.
Kidney caveat: normal kidneys do not need protein restriction, but eGFR <30 or medical kidney disease changes the equation. Get clinician guidance.
contraindications: Advanced kidney disease (eGFR <30) — discuss with nephrology
side_effects: Mild GI upset with rapid increases
Higher per-day calorie load if not displacing other macros

name: Psyllium Husk Fiber
one_liner: Soluble fiber with FDA-recognized heart-disease risk reduction claim. Lowers LDL cholesterol and improves bowel function. Genuinely useful, often overlooked.
plain_summary: Soluble fiber (the kind that turns into gel in your gut, like the texture of cooked oatmeal). About 10g per day lowers your LDL cholesterol by ~9% — comparable to a low-dose statin. The FDA officially approves the heart-disease-prevention claim. Also fixes constipation and diarrhea (yes, both), and slightly smooths blood-sugar spikes after meals. Cheap, well-tolerated after the first couple weeks.
cost_signal: $8-15/month for generic psyllium husk powder (Now Foods, Yerba Prima) or unflavored Metamucil. Sweetened/flavored versions slightly more.
who_should_consider: Elevated LDL not yet on a statin
On a statin but not at target
Constipation or alternating bowel habits (IBS)
Don't eat 25-35g fiber/day from food
Type 2 diabetes — improves post-prandial glucose
who_should_skip: Already eat ≥35g fiber/day from beans, oats, vegetables — diet wins
Esophageal stricture or swallowing difficulty (choking risk)
Have to take many medications throughout the day — timing becomes complex
verdict: Underrated. Cheap (Metamucil generic <$10/mo), well-tolerated after initial bloating, and has FDA-grade evidence for cardiovascular risk reduction. Equivalent LDL reduction to a low-dose statin. If you don't eat 25-35g daily fiber from food, psyllium is probably the highest-value supplement you can take. Take separately from medications.
what_to_do: Start with 5g (1 teaspoon) once daily mixed in 250 mL water, drunk immediately. Increase to 7-15g/day in divided doses (typically before meals). ALWAYS take with full glass of water. Take medications 2 hours before or after psyllium (it binds them). Expect 1-2 weeks of gas/bloating during gut adaptation. Generic Metamucil (sugar-free version) is cheapest; the orange-flavor sweetened version contains sugar/artificial sweetener.
contraindications: Take separately from medications (binds them — separate by 2 hours)
side_effects: Esophageal obstruction if taken without enough water

name: Pterostilbene
one_liner: Methylated resveratrol analog with better bioavailability. Limited human longevity data.
plain_summary: Pterostilbene is essentially resveratrol's better-absorbed cousin. Marketed as a longevity supplement; the human data is thin. The whole 'polyphenol supplement' thesis has not aged well. Skip it. Eat blueberries (where pterostilbene naturally occurs in tiny amounts), grapes, nuts, dark chocolate, EVOO — and call it a day.
who_should_consider: Probably nobody, given the cost-to-evidence ratio.
who_should_skip: Most people. On anticoagulants without medical advice.
verdict: Skip. The polyphenol-via-pill thesis is weak. Get them from food.
what_to_do: Honestly: skip the supplement. Eat the foods (blueberries, grapes, almonds) instead.
If you must: 50–250 mg/day, but expect little.
If you also take resveratrol, you don't need both — same general pathway.
May raise LDL slightly at higher doses; check lipids if you take it long-term.
Avoid if on anticoagulants (theoretical bleeding risk).

name: Quercetin
one_liner: Flavonoid commonly paired with dasatinib in senolytic protocols.
plain_summary: Quercetin is the famous flavonoid in apples, onions, and capers. As a senolytic, it only works combined with a chemo drug called dasatinib (D+Q protocol used in research). Solo quercetin pills are unimpressive. The food-source benefit is real — eat apples, onions, kale. The pill: skip.
cost_signal: $10–$25/month for capsules. Onions and apples are cheaper and better.
who_should_consider: Pretty much nobody as a supplement. Everyone for the food sources.
who_should_skip: On anticoagulants, cyclosporine, or multiple prescriptions without checking interactions.
verdict: Without dasatinib, quercetin alone is barely interesting. The food-source value is real (eat apples, onions). Pill form: skip.
what_to_do: Eat the foods: apples (with skin), red onions, capers, kale, dark berries. Daily.
If you're set on supplementing: 500–1000 mg/day with food. Don't expect much from quercetin alone.
The D+Q senolytic protocol is research-only; not a do-it-yourself thing. Dasatinib is a chemo drug with serious side effects.
If you want a senolytic bet that works as a single agent: fisetin has more evidence (separate entry).
Watch for drug interactions: quercetin inhibits CYP3A4 — collisions with statins, BP meds, antidepressants are possible.
Skip if on anticoagulants or cyclosporine.

name: Rapamycin (Sirolimus)
one_liner: The most consistent mammalian-lifespan-extending drug we have. Human longevity data still pending.
plain_summary: Rapamycin is the only drug proven to extend lifespan across nearly every animal tested, including old mice. In humans, we know it tunes down the cellular signal that drives growth and ramps up your body's recycling system. Whether it actually adds years to human lives is still an open question — the trials haven't read out yet.
cost_signal: $30–$150/month depending on pharmacy and dose. Telehealth longevity clinics charge $200–500 in addition for consult + bloodwork.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 with no contraindications who want to act on the strongest mammalian longevity signal we have, accept that human proof is still pending, and have access to a doctor willing to prescribe and monitor.
who_should_skip: Anyone with active infections, planning surgery, pregnant or trying to conceive, severely immunocompromised, with poorly controlled diabetes, or unwilling to do regular bloodwork. Also: don't take it just because someone on a podcast does.
verdict: Strongest mammalian lifespan signal of any drug, period. Human longevity benefit is plausible but not yet proven. If you can find a longevity-literate physician, intermittent low-dose protocols are increasingly common off-label. Self-medication is unwise — interactions and dosing matter.
what_to_do: Don't self-medicate. Find a longevity-literate physician (functional med, anti-aging clinic, or a primary doc willing to prescribe off-label).
Typical off-label longevity protocol: 5–8 mg once weekly, NOT daily. Daily transplant doses cause immunosuppression.
Take it on an empty stomach, same day each week. Most protocols cycle it or take breaks every few months.
Get bloodwork before starting and every 3–6 months: lipids, fasting glucose, HbA1c, CBC. Watch for mouth ulcers (most common side effect).
Pause it 2 weeks before any surgery and skip dosing if you're fighting an infection.
If you're trying to conceive, on chemo, or have an active infection — don't take it.
side_effects: Mouth ulcers (most common at higher dose)

name: Red yeast rice
one_liner: Lowers LDL cholesterol because it contains a low dose of lovastatin (a statin). Real effect, real risk, opaque dosing — most people would do better with a real statin under a doctor's supervision.
plain_summary: Red yeast rice (RYR) is fermented rice that contains monacolin K — a molecule that is chemically identical to lovastatin, a prescription statin. So 'red yeast rice lowers cholesterol' is true: it's a low, unregulated dose of a real cholesterol drug. The CSPPT trial in China (n=4,870 with prior heart attack) showed a Chinese RYR product reduced cardiovascular events similarly to a low-dose statin. Sounds great. The catch: dosing varies wildly between brands (one analysis found 100x differences), some products contain a contaminant called citrinin that's toxic to kidneys, and you get all the statin side effects — muscle pain, liver enzyme rise — without the medical oversight a real statin prescription comes with. If your LDL or ApoB is high enough that lowering it matters, ask your doctor about a real statin. RYR's main role is as a fallback if someone refuses prescription medicine but will take a 'natural' alternative.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated LDL/ApoB who refuse prescription statins for non-medical reasons but accept 'natural' supplements, AND who will use a quality-controlled brand AND will get standard side-effect monitoring. Honest assessment: this is a small population. For everyone else: a real statin is better.
who_should_skip: Anyone willing to take a prescription statin (more dose-controlled, monitored, often free or cheap). Pregnancy and breastfeeding — statins (including RYR's monacolin K) are contraindicated. Active liver disease. Anyone on CYP3A4 inhibitors (grapefruit, ketoconazole, erythromycin, clarithromycin, certain HIV drugs) — interaction can raise monacolin K to dangerous levels. People with kidney disease without confirmed citrinin-free product. Anyone already on a statin (additive toxicity). History of statin-induced muscle damage.
verdict: Effective at LDL lowering because it contains a low-dose statin. The right answer for almost everyone with elevated LDL/ApoB is a real prescription statin: known dose, monitored, often cheaper, lower contamination risk. RYR is a fallback for the small population that refuses prescription medicine but accepts 'natural' alternatives — and even then, only with a quality-controlled brand and standard monitoring. The marketing positions RYR as a 'safer alternative' to statins; it is not safer, it is just less regulated.
what_to_do: If your LDL or ApoB is elevated and treatment is indicated, get a real statin prescription rather than RYR. The dose is known, the product is consistent, the side-effect monitoring is standard. If you specifically refuse prescription medicine but accept a 'natural' product: choose a brand that publishes monacolin K content and tests for citrinin (Mannarino & Pirro 2018 evaluated commercial brands; many failed). Typical effective dose 1.2–2.4 g/day RYR providing 5–10 mg monacolin K. Side-effect monitoring (muscle pain, liver enzymes) should match what a clinician would order for a low-dose statin: CK if symptomatic, ALT/AST at 3 months. Avoid grapefruit. Stop if muscle pain develops.
contraindications: Pregnancy, breastfeeding (statin class is contraindicated). Active liver disease. Concurrent prescription statin (additive toxicity). Concurrent CYP3A4 inhibitors (grapefruit juice, ketoconazole, erythromycin, clarithromycin, ritonavir, cyclosporine). History of statin-induced rhabdomyolysis or severe myopathy. Children (no safety data).
side_effects: Same as statins: muscle pain or weakness (~1–5%), liver enzyme elevation (rare), GI upset, headache. Rhabdomyolysis is rare but documented, especially with high doses or CYP3A4 inhibitor co-medication. Citrinin (where present in product) can damage kidneys with long-term exposure.

name: Resistance Training
one_liner: Lean mass and strength are independent mortality predictors. Both decline without training.
plain_summary: Lifting heavy things — squats, deadlifts, presses, rows — twice a week is non-negotiable past 40. Muscle and strength are independent predictors of how long you live. They both decline relentlessly without training, and that decline is what eventually steals your independence in old age.
cost_signal: Gym membership $30–$80/month. Or one good barbell + plates + rack at home: $800–$2000 one-time.
who_should_consider: Everyone. Especially anyone over 40, anyone losing strength or mobility, anyone with osteopenia or family history of frailty.
who_should_skip: Nobody — but anyone with cardiac disease, recent surgery, or active injury should clear with a doctor and ideally work with a physical therapist or qualified coach first.
verdict: Non-negotiable for healthy aging. The strongest interventions for the back third of life: maintain muscle, maintain strength, maintain power. Skip squats and you'll regret it at 80.
what_to_do: Train 2–3 times per week, 45–75 min per session. Non-negotiable.
Focus on compound lifts: squat (or leg press), deadlift (or hinge), bench/overhead press, pull-ups (or rows). These hit the most muscle for the least time.
Progress gradually. Add a small amount of weight or one rep when last week's weight feels manageable. This 'progressive overload' is the entire game.
If you're new: hire a coach for 4–8 sessions to learn form. Money well spent — bad form for years is worse than no training.
Don't skip legs. Lower-body strength tracks closely with mortality and fall risk.
Eat enough protein: ~1.6 g per kg of bodyweight per day (more if older). Train fed; sleep 7–9h.
Soreness is fine. Sharp pain is not — back off and reassess.

name: Resveratrol
one_liner: Famous red-wine polyphenol. Hype outpaced evidence.
plain_summary: Resveratrol is the famous red-wine compound that started the modern longevity-supplement industry. After 20 years of research, the evidence in humans is genuinely disappointing. The original sirtuin-activation story turned out to be a lab artifact. The bioavailability is terrible. Skip it. If you want polyphenols, eat berries, dark chocolate, EVOO, and drink green tea.
cost_signal: $15–$50/month wasted.
who_should_consider: Probably nobody.
who_should_skip: Most people. Anyone using it as a substitute for actual lifestyle changes.
verdict: The poster child for hype outpacing evidence. Skip it. If you want polyphenols, eat berries, dark chocolate, EVOO and green tea — same molecular family, food matrix included.
what_to_do: Skip the supplement.
If you want polyphenols, get them from food: berries, dark chocolate (70%+), EVOO, green tea, red onions, walnuts. Same molecular family, with the food matrix that actually works.
Don't drink wine 'for the resveratrol' — the alcohol cost (cancer, sleep, BP) outweighs any polyphenol benefit a hundredfold.
If you're taking resveratrol now: it's not dangerous to continue, just probably not doing much.
Avoid high doses if on anticoagulants or hormone-sensitive cancer history (mild estrogenic activity at high doses).

name: Saturated Fat Replacement
one_liner: The useful version is not “eat less fat.” Replace butter, cream, cheese, and fatty processed meat with unsaturated fats and high-fiber foods.
plain_summary: Saturated fat advice is confusing because reduction only helps if the replacement is better. Replacing butter/cream/cheese/fatty processed meat with olive oil, nuts, avocado, fish, legumes, oats, and whole grains lowers LDL/ApoB. Replacing saturated fat with refined starch or sugar is much less useful.
cost_signal: Depends on swaps. Olive oil/nuts cost more; legumes/oats cost less.
who_should_consider: Anyone with elevated LDL/ApoB, family history of cardiovascular disease, or high intake of butter, cheese, cream, coconut oil, fatty meat, or processed meat.
who_should_skip: People with already very low ApoB and low saturated-fat intake do not need to obsess.
verdict: For ApoB, replacement matters. Swap saturated fat toward unsaturated fat and soluble-fiber foods; do not just reduce fat and add refined carbohydrates.
what_to_do: Measure ApoB or LDL-C before and after changes if this is your target.
Replace butter with extra-virgin olive oil most of the time.
Reduce cheese/cream-heavy meals if ApoB is high.
Use legumes, oats, nuts, fish, yogurt, and lean proteins as defaults.
Avoid coconut oil as a health food if LDL/ApoB is high. It raises LDL in many people.
side_effects: Can be overdone if nuts/oils add calories without replacing anything

name: Sauna (Finnish-Style)
one_liner: Strong dose-response cohort data on CV and all-cause mortality.
plain_summary: If you can get to a Finnish sauna 3–4 times a week, do it. The data linking frequent sauna use to lower heart-disease and overall death rates is some of the strongest non-drug evidence on this site. Hot tub may be a partial substitute. Cost-of-life from doing it: roughly zero.
cost_signal: Free if your gym has one. Home sauna: $3,000–$10,000+ one-time. Hot tub: $4,000–$15,000.
who_should_consider: Pretty much everyone with access. Especially people with hypertension or moderate cardiovascular risk (after medical clearance).
who_should_skip: Severe aortic stenosis, pregnancy (heat caution), active fever/infection, severe orthostatic hypotension, recent heart attack.
verdict: Among the strongest non-pharma interventions on this list. Dose-response is striking. Even if causality is partial, the downside is small. If you have sauna access, use it 3-4x/week. Hot tub may approximate.
what_to_do: Aim for 4 sessions per week of 20 minutes each at 80–100°C (175–210°F). Frequency drives the benefit more than length.
Hydrate well before and after. Add electrolytes if you sweat heavily.
Cool down gradually. Sit for a minute before standing (orthostatic dizziness is the main risk).
Don't use a sauna while drunk. Don't use one alone if you have any cardiovascular history.
Infrared saunas are easier to install at home but evidence is weaker than for traditional Finnish. Use traditional if you have access.
Hot tub at 40°C / 104°F for 30+ minutes has emerging similar-but-smaller signal. Better than nothing.
If you have heart disease, control hypertension, or take BP meds, talk to your doctor before starting.

name: SGLT2 Inhibitors (Empagliflozin, Dapagliflozin)
one_liner: Cardio-renal protection in diabetes, heart failure, and CKD — even without diabetes.
plain_summary: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin / Jardiance, dapagliflozin / Farxiga) are originally diabetes drugs that turned out to dramatically reduce death from heart failure and slow kidney decline — even in people who don't have diabetes. They're among the most exciting cardiology drugs of the last decade. Off-label longevity use is increasingly discussed but should involve a doctor familiar with the class.
cost_signal: $300–$600/month list price; insurance often covers for diabetes/heart-failure indications. Few generic options yet.
who_should_consider: People with type 2 diabetes, heart failure (any ejection fraction), CKD with proteinuria. Off-label: metabolic syndrome with cardiovascular risk after physician discussion.
who_should_skip: Type 1 diabetes. Recurrent UTIs or genital fungal infections. Severe renal impairment. Frail elderly with hypotension risk.
verdict: Among the most exciting cardiology drugs of the past decade. Direct longevity-relevant outcomes in non-diabetics. Off-label longevity use is increasingly discussed but should involve a physician familiar with the class.
what_to_do: Ask your doctor — this is prescription. Easier to get if you have type 2 diabetes, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
Empagliflozin 10–25 mg/day or dapagliflozin 5–10 mg/day. Once daily, morning.
Drink more water than usual. Mild diuretic effect is normal but dehydration is a real risk, especially in summer or with stomach bugs.
Practice good genital hygiene — these slightly raise yeast-infection risk in both sexes.
Stop the drug if you're going to be NPO for surgery (last dose 3 days before). Restart after.
Don't take it if you have type 1 diabetes — risk of euglycemic ketoacidosis.
Brief illness with vomiting/diarrhea: pause it temporarily.

name: Sleep (7-9h, regularity, deep+REM)
one_liner: Chronically <6h or >9h sleep both increase mortality. Regularity matters as much as duration.
plain_summary: If you're sleeping 5–6 hours regularly and accepting it, fixing that is the single biggest health upgrade you can make — bigger than any pill on this site. 7–9 hours, consistent timing, and a quiet dark cool room. If you snore or wake unrested, get screened for sleep apnea — that's the most under-diagnosed killer in this whole space.
cost_signal: $0 to start. Sleep study $200–$1500 (often insurance-covered). Decent mattress + blackout curtains $500–$2000 one-time.
who_should_consider: Everyone. If you're chronically under 6h or have any unrested-wake symptoms, this is your highest-leverage intervention.
who_should_skip: Nobody. People with chronic insomnia or shift work need targeted help (CBT-I beats sleeping pills long-term).
verdict: If you only sleep 5-6h regularly, fixing that is more impactful than any supplement on this list. Regularity matters as much as duration. Get screened for sleep apnea if you snore or wake unrested.
what_to_do: Pick a bedtime and stick to it. Variation under 30 minutes night-to-night. Regularity rivals duration in the data.
Aim for 7–9 hours in bed. If you can't function on 7, your sleep quality is the problem, not your need.
Cool, dark, quiet room. 18–20°C / 65–68°F. Blackout curtains or eye mask. Fan/white noise if needed.
No screens 30–60 min before bed. Or use them with brightness down + blue-light filter.
No caffeine after lunch. No alcohol within 3 hours of bed (it wrecks REM sleep even if it 'helps' you fall asleep).
Get morning sunlight within an hour of waking — anchors your circadian rhythm.
If you snore loudly, wake up gasping, or feel unrested despite 8h: get a sleep study. Sleep apnea is the silent killer here.

name: Social Connection
one_liner: Loneliness and social isolation increase mortality on the order of smoking 15 cigarettes/day.
plain_summary: Loneliness and social isolation are as bad for your lifespan as smoking 15 cigarettes a day. Possibly the most under-rated longevity intervention of all — and nobody sells it. Invest in friendships and family the way you'd invest in exercise: deliberately, consistently, and treat it as essential, not optional.
cost_signal: Free to mostly free. The investment is time and attention.
who_should_consider: Everyone. Especially anyone who feels chronically lonely, lives alone, recently retired, recently moved, or recently lost a partner.
who_should_skip: Nobody.
verdict: Among the most powerful longevity 'interventions' that nobody sells. Invest in relationships. Schedule it like exercise. The effect size dwarfs most things in this list.
what_to_do: Schedule social contact like exercise. Weekly call with a friend. Monthly dinner with a group. Quarterly trip with old friends.
Diversity matters more than depth. A wide network beats one tight bond — strong-tie + weak-tie variety is what predicts mortality outcomes.
Show up in person when possible. Video calls help. Texting alone doesn't deliver the full benefit.
Join something with regular meetings: club, class, sport, religious group, volunteer org. Built-in repetition is what social cognition needs.
If you're isolated and don't know where to start: pick one person you've lost touch with and reach out this week. Then again next month. Then add a second person.
If you live alone and are over 65, treat this as a medical priority. Loneliness in older adults is a mortality multiplier.

name: Sodium / Potassium Balance
one_liner: Lower sodium and higher food-based potassium reliably lower blood pressure. The effect is boring, measurable, and highly actionable.
plain_summary: For blood pressure, sodium and potassium are the nutrition levers most people can actually measure. Less restaurant/packaged food lowers sodium; more beans, potatoes, greens, yogurt, fruit, and nuts raises potassium. The goal is not electrolyte biohacking. It is moving blood pressure down.
cost_signal: $0-10/month. Salt substitutes are cheap; produce/legumes may change grocery mix.
who_should_consider: Adults with elevated blood pressure, high processed-food intake, or low fruit/vegetable/legume intake.
who_should_skip: People with CKD, hyperkalemia, potassium-sparing diuretics, ACE inhibitors/ARBs, or heart failure should ask a clinician before potassium salts.
verdict: If blood pressure is high, sodium reduction plus potassium-rich foods is one of the highest-certainty nutrition moves. Use food first; be careful with potassium chloride salt substitutes if kidney function or medications make potassium risky.
what_to_do: Estimate sodium for one normal week. Restaurant meals and packaged foods are usually the source.
Aim for under 2,300 mg/day; under 1,500 mg/day if hypertension and tolerable.
Add potassium from food: beans, lentils, potatoes, leafy greens, yogurt, bananas, citrus, nuts.
Consider potassium-chloride salt substitute only if kidney function is normal and medications allow it.
Use home BP readings, not vibes, to assess effect.
contraindications: Unmonitored ACE inhibitor or ARB use with potassium salt substitutes
side_effects: Food tastes flat for 2-3 weeks while palate adapts
Potassium salts can cause dangerous hyperkalemia in susceptible people

name: Soy Foods (tofu, tempeh, edamame)
one_liner: A useful protein-and-fiber food swap. Modestly lowers LDL and helps replace red/processed meat without losing protein.
plain_summary: Soy foods are not magic and not endocrine poison. Tofu, tempeh, edamame, and soy milk provide high-quality plant protein, fiber/isoflavones, and a practical way to replace red or processed meat. The LDL effect is modest; the food-swap value is larger.
cost_signal: $5-20/month depending on use. Tofu/edamame are usually cheap proteins.
who_should_consider: People reducing red meat, people wanting plant protein, people with elevated LDL/ApoB who need practical swaps.
who_should_skip: Soy allergy. People on highly restricted thyroid medication timing should separate soy from levothyroxine dosing.
verdict: Soy foods are a good longevity food when they replace worse proteins. Choose tofu, tempeh, edamame, unsweetened soy milk. Do not infer the same from highly processed soy junk food.
what_to_do: Use tofu/tempeh/edamame 2-5 times per week as a protein default.
Choose unsweetened soy milk if using milk alternatives; it has better protein than almond/oat milk.
Replace processed meat or cheese-heavy meals, not vegetables.
If on levothyroxine, keep dosing consistent and separate from soy-heavy meals by several hours.
side_effects: Medication timing issue with thyroid hormone absorption

name: Spermidine
one_liner: Polyamine that induces autophagy. Strong cohort mortality signal; cognition RCT results have been mixed.
plain_summary: A natural compound that helps your cells clean themselves up. People who eat lots of it (wheat germ, cheese, mushrooms) seem to live longer — but the study giving it as a pill for memory didn't show a benefit. Diet > pills here.
who_should_consider: Anyone with a low-polyamine diet (low whole grains, no aged cheese, no fermented foods). Worth raising dietary intake.
who_should_skip: People expecting a cognitive boost from capsules — the best RCT we have was null.
verdict: Eat the foods (wheat germ, aged cheese, soybeans, mushrooms). Pill supplementation evidence is weaker than the hype — the largest cognitive RCT was null. If the BRUNECK food-intake signal is causal, capsules at typical doses likely don't replicate it.
what_to_do: Eat more wheat germ, aged cheese, soybeans, mushrooms. The pill is probably not worth it at current doses unless cost is no object.
contraindications: No formal ones; people with severe wheat allergies should avoid wheat germ extract products.
side_effects: Generally well tolerated in trials. GI upset reported rarely.

name: Statins
one_liner: Single best-validated drug class for reducing cardiovascular mortality.
plain_summary: If your ApoB or LDL cholesterol is elevated, especially if you already have any sign of heart-artery plaque, statins are the single best-proven longevity drug we have. They reduce heart attack and death rates substantially. The 'muscle ache' fear is real but small and often goes away or can be managed by switching brands. Don't avoid them on vibes — get tested and decide with your doctor.
cost_signal: $5–$30/month generic. Among the cheapest interventions on this list.
who_should_consider: Anyone with elevated ApoB, established atherosclerosis, prior heart attack, diabetes with cardiovascular risk factors, or strong family history of early heart disease.
who_should_skip: Active liver disease, pregnancy/breastfeeding. Low-risk patients with normal ApoB and zero CAC may have a large NNT (number needed to treat) — discuss tradeoffs.
verdict: If you have elevated ApoB or established atherosclerosis, statins are the single best-validated longevity drug we have. Not a 'natural' alternative — they're a real win. If you're low risk, NNT is large; discuss with your physician.
what_to_do: Get an ApoB blood test (better than standard LDL) and a coronary calcium score (CAC) if you're 40+. These are the best risk-stratifying tests we have.
If your ApoB is high or your CAC is non-zero, talk to your doctor about a statin. Atorvastatin and rosuvastatin are the most commonly used.
Start at a moderate dose. Recheck lipids and liver enzymes at 6–12 weeks.
If you get muscle aches: try switching to a different statin (rosuvastatin and pravastatin tend to be better tolerated). Often works.
Combine with lifestyle: diet, exercise, sleep — they multiply the benefit.
Don't stop without talking to your doctor. Stopping statins after years on them carries real cardiac risk.
Avoid grapefruit juice with simvastatin (interaction).
side_effects: Muscle symptoms reported in 5-10% but ~90% nocebo per SAMSON N-of-1 trial
Small absolute increase in new-onset T2DM (~0.1%/year, offset by CV mortality benefit)
Liver enzyme elevation (clinically significant in <1%; routine monitoring debated)
Rare rhabdomyolysis (<0.01%/year, watch for severe pain + dark urine)

name: Sulforaphane (Broccoli Sprouts)
one_liner: Most potent natural Nrf2 activator. Mainly biomarker data; intriguing autism trial.
plain_summary: Sulforaphane is the active compound that makes broccoli sprouts unusually healthy. It's the most potent natural activator we know of for a master switch (Nrf2) that turns on your body's own antioxidant and detox systems. Eat broccoli sprouts. Grow them in a jar on your counter — it costs nothing.
cost_signal: $1–$5/month if growing your own. $30–$60/month for high-quality supplements.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone. Cheapest and easiest natural longevity intervention on this site.
who_should_skip: People on warfarin should mention regular cruciferous intake to their doctor (vitamin K content, not sulforaphane, but worth flagging).
verdict: Eat broccoli sprouts. Cheap, easy to grow, no need to supplement. Among the most potent natural longevity-relevant phytochemicals.
what_to_do: Eat 30–100 g of broccoli sprouts daily. That's a small handful.
Grow them yourself: $5 of seeds makes weeks of sprouts. Soak overnight, drain, rinse twice daily, harvest in 4–5 days. Glass jar with mesh lid works.
Eat them raw or barely warmed — heat destroys the enzyme (myrosinase) that activates sulforaphane.
Add to salads, sandwiches, smoothies. Mustard seed powder boosts the conversion if you eat them with cooked broccoli.
Supplement option: 'TrueBroc' or similar broccoli sprout extracts if you can't grow. Look for 'glucoraphanin + active myrosinase' — without the enzyme, the supplement is less effective.
Mild GI discomfort at high intake; back off if so.
side_effects: Possible thyroid effect at very high intake (unlikely)

name: Sun Exposure / Circadian Alignment
one_liner: Daily morning sunlight anchors circadian rhythm. UV trade-off: balance D production with skin cancer risk.
plain_summary: Get bright light in your eyes within an hour of waking up. That single habit anchors your body's daily rhythm — better sleep at night, sharper alertness during the day, more stable mood. The skin cancer warnings about sun exposure are real but often overshoot: total sun avoidance has its own mortality cost. Aim for moderate, not zero.
cost_signal: Free (going outside). $50–$200 for a SAD lamp if you need indoor backup.
who_should_consider: Everyone. Especially indoor workers, anyone with poor sleep, anyone with seasonal affective patterns, shift workers.
who_should_skip: Photosensitivity disorders. On photosensitizing medications without skin protection. Pale skin types should still get sun but more cautiously.
verdict: Get bright light in your eyes within an hour of waking. Get some skin sun without burning. The 'sun-cancer' frame missed the all-cause picture.
what_to_do: Within 30–60 minutes of waking: get outside for 5–15 minutes. Cloudy still works (cloudy outdoors is brighter than indoor).
Don't wear sunglasses for that morning window — your eyes need the light. Hat is fine.
On dark winter mornings: a 10,000-lux SAD lamp (look for 'bright light therapy') is a reasonable substitute — 15–30 min while you have coffee.
During the day: get bright light periodically. A walk at lunch is better than coffee.
Skin sun: 10–20 min of midday sun on arms and legs without sunscreen, a few times a week, before SPF goes on. Don't burn — burning is what drives most skin cancer risk.
Avoid bright light in the 2–3 hours before bed. Dim your environment, use warm-tinted bulbs, dim screens.
If you're on photosensitizing meds (some antibiotics, retinoids, etc.) be cautious — check with your pharmacist.
side_effects: Skin cancer risk if excessive UV

name: Taurine
one_liner: Amino acid with strong 2023 mouse lifespan signal. Human longevity data uncertain.
plain_summary: A 2023 study made headlines: taurine supplementation extended lifespan dramatically in mice and improved many aging markers. Human data is more mixed — some cohort studies suggest a benefit, others don't. It's cheap, well-tolerated, and has a real biological rationale. Modest hedge; don't expect mouse-scale results in humans.
cost_signal: $10–$25/month for bulk powder.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50, particularly with cardiovascular risk factors or low energy. Vegans and vegetarians (you get less from food).
who_should_skip: Bipolar disorder. People on bromocriptine. Pregnancy (no data).
verdict: Big mouse signal, conflicting human signal. Cheap, well-tolerated, plausible upside. Modest hedge. Don't expect mouse-scale longevity in humans.
what_to_do: Dose: 3–6 g per day, split into 2–3 doses with meals. The mouse study used the equivalent of ~3 g for a human.
Powder form is cheapest — measure with a kitchen scale. It's nearly tasteless.
Pair with magnesium for theoretical synergy in cardiovascular and nervous-system effects.
Don't get it from energy drinks — the doses are tiny and the sugar/caffeine load defeats the purpose.
Skip if you have bipolar disorder (theoretical concern about effects on mood regulation).
Reassess after 3–6 months; if no biomarker or subjective improvement, reasonable to drop it.

name: Time-Restricted Eating
one_liner: Compressed eating window. Modest metabolic benefit; mortality data thin.
plain_summary: Eating in an 8–10 hour window each day (skip breakfast OR skip dinner) gives modest metabolic benefits and many people find it useful as a discipline structure. It's not magic — total calorie quality and quantity still dominate. There's a slight signal that eating earlier in the day is metabolically better than eating later.
cost_signal: Free.
who_should_consider: People who like rules and structure, people with mild metabolic concerns, people who already snack late at night and want a wind-down cue.
who_should_skip: Eating disorder history (any). Pregnancy. Athletes during heavy training blocks. Type 1 diabetes (medication timing complications). Underweight individuals.
verdict: Probably useful as eating-discipline structure. Not magic — calorie quality and quantity dominate. Skipping breakfast is fine; skipping dinner may be metabolically better.
what_to_do: Pick an 8–10 hour eating window and keep it consistent. Common choice: 11am–7pm or 10am–6pm.
If possible, skip dinner instead of breakfast — early eating is metabolically slightly better.
