Drink water, plain tea, or black coffee outside the window. Anything with calories breaks the fast.
Don't compress your normal calories into a binge. Aim for the same total food, different timing.
Keep protein intake high (1.6 g/kg bodyweight). Tighter windows risk muscle loss without enough protein.
Don't combine with extreme calorie restriction unless you know what you're doing.
If you have an eating disorder history, skip this entirely. The structure can become disordered fast.
side_effects: Possible muscle loss if protein intake is low

name: TMG (Trimethylglycine / Betaine)
one_liner: Methyl donor often paired with NMN/NR to support methylation balance.
plain_summary: TMG (trimethylglycine, also called betaine) is a methyl-donor compound often paired with NMN or NR. The logic: NAD+ precursors burn through your body's methyl groups, and TMG replaces them. Reasonable to take if you're stacking NAD+ precursors; not magical alone. Naturally found in beets and spinach.
who_should_consider: People stacking NMN or NR. People with elevated homocysteine on routine bloodwork.
who_should_skip: People not on NAD+ precursors and with normal homocysteine. People with elevated LDL (use lowest effective dose).
verdict: Reasonable to take 500-1000mg/day if stacking with NAD+ precursors, but not magical alone.
what_to_do: If you take NMN or NR: pair with 500–1000 mg TMG/day. Take same time as the NAD+ precursor.
If you don't take NMN/NR: probably skip TMG unless your homocysteine is elevated.
Powder form is cheap and tasteless; capsules are convenient.
Beets and spinach are natural sources — useful background but not enough to replace a deliberate methyl-donor supplement when stacking NAD+ precursors.
Watch your LDL cholesterol — TMG at high doses (>3 g/day) has been shown to raise LDL slightly.
If you have high LDL, use lower doses (500 mg) or skip.
Mild fishy body odor is a rare side effect; back off if it happens.

name: Testosterone Replacement (TRT) for hypogonadism
one_liner: For men with confirmed hypogonadism (low T + symptoms): improves muscle mass, mood, sexual function. TRAVERSE 2023 NEJM established CV safety. Not for general 'low T' marketing.
plain_summary: Testosterone declines with age in men — about 1% per year after 40 — but the rate varies enormously between individuals. The clinical question is when low testosterone is causing symptoms that warrant treatment. TRT done correctly (for men with confirmed biochemical hypogonadism plus symptoms) consistently improves muscle mass, sexual function, mood, and bone density. The historical concern that TRT might increase cardiovascular events was substantially addressed by the TRAVERSE trial (Lincoff 2023 NEJM, n=5,246), which found no increase in major cardiovascular events. The bigger problem is over-prescription: men with normal-range testosterone or borderline-low values without symptoms get prescribed TRT for vague concerns, which doesn't improve outcomes and creates lifetime medication dependence. TRT is real medicine for real hypogonadism, not a longevity supplement.
who_should_consider: Men with confirmed biochemical hypogonadism (two morning T levels in deficient range) AND symptoms. Particularly relevant for men with surgical/medical hypogonadism (cancer survivors after orchiectomy, congenital causes, pituitary disease). Men with persistent post-pubertal testosterone deficiency.
who_should_skip: Men with normal-range testosterone seeking 'optimization'. Men trying to conceive (suppresses fertility; can take 6+ months to recover after stopping). Men with active prostate cancer (relative contraindication). Untreated severe obstructive sleep apnea (TRT can worsen). Men with hematocrit >54% baseline. Men with breast cancer. Men with severe untreated CV disease. Men with elevated baseline PSA without urology evaluation. Men with primary hypogonadism wanting to preserve fertility (consider hCG/clomiphene alternatives that maintain testicular function).
verdict: Real medicine for confirmed hypogonadism with symptoms. Not a longevity supplement. The TRAVERSE 2023 trial established cardiovascular safety. Diagnose properly: two morning T levels + consistent symptoms. Don't prescribe to men with normal T 'for optimization' — that's medicalisation, not medicine. If you're considering TRT, work with a clinician who follows the Endocrine Society guideline, not a clinic that prescribes TRT to nearly everyone who walks in. Get fertility plans clarified before starting (or use hCG/clomiphene alternatives if fertility matters).
what_to_do: Diagnosis FIRST: at least two morning total testosterone levels (8-10am, fasting if possible) below 264 ng/dL (Endocrine Society threshold) or 300 ng/dL (more conservative threshold) AND symptoms consistent with hypogonadism (decreased libido, ED, decreased muscle mass, fatigue, depression, decreased morning erections). Symptoms alone don't warrant TRT; biochemistry alone doesn't warrant TRT. Both together do. Common formulations: testosterone cypionate IM 100-200mg every 1-2 weeks; testosterone gel 25-50mg/day; testosterone enanthate; subcutaneous pellets every 3-6 months. Monitoring: total testosterone, hematocrit (target <54%), PSA, lipids, mood, sexual function — at 3 months, 6 months, then annually. Stop if no symptomatic improvement after 6 months at adequate dose. NOT for: men with high-normal testosterone seeking optimization; men trying to conceive (suppresses spermatogenesis); men with active prostate cancer or untreated severe sleep apnea.
contraindications: Active prostate cancer. Severe untreated obstructive sleep apnea. Hematocrit >54% baseline. Breast cancer. Active or recent CV event (TRAVERSE excluded recent ACS). Untreated polycythemia. Pregnancy in partner trying to conceive (T can transfer via skin contact with gels).
side_effects: Erythrocytosis (HCT >54%, requires phlebotomy or dose reduction). Acne. Mild scalp hair thinning in genetically predisposed. Sleep apnea worsening (treat OSA first). Modest LDL increase, HDL decrease. Mild fluid retention. Possible AF and PE signal (TRAVERSE). Suppressed spermatogenesis (reversible but slow).

name: Ultra-Processed Food Reduction
one_liner: The most practical weight, glucose, and cardiometabolic nutrition rule: make packaged hyper-palatable food the exception, not the default.
plain_summary: Ultra-processed foods are engineered to be easy to overeat: refined starch/fat/salt/sugar combinations, low fiber, fast eating rate, high energy density. The strongest trial evidence shows people eat hundreds more calories per day when offered ultra-processed diets even when macros are matched. For longevity, this is the food-environment lever.
cost_signal: Often cost-neutral if replaced with legumes, oats, eggs, yogurt, frozen vegetables, potatoes, and simple meals.
who_should_consider: Anyone with weight gain, insulin resistance, high triglycerides, fatty liver, cravings, or a snack-heavy food environment.
who_should_skip: No one needs ultra-processed food as a default; athletes may still use specific sports foods around training.
verdict: This is less about moral purity than food physics. Reduce the foods that make passive overeating easy and cardiometabolic markers usually move in the right direction.
what_to_do: Pick the top 3 ultra-processed defaults you eat most often: packaged snacks, sweets, refined breakfast foods, fast food, sweet drinks.
Replace with boring defaults: Greek yogurt, oats, legumes, eggs, potatoes, fruit, nuts, simple sandwiches, frozen vegetables, canned fish.
Keep treats deliberate and portioned, not open-ended in the house.
Use protein and fiber at breakfast to reduce later snack drive.
Do not chase perfection. The target is default environment, not never eating pizza again.
side_effects: Social friction if most meals are takeout

name: Urolithin A
one_liner: Postbiotic that induces mitophagy. Real human muscle-function data.
plain_summary: Urolithin A is one of the better-evidenced longevity supplements: real human studies showing improved muscle endurance and mitochondrial function. Your gut bacteria normally make it from pomegranate and walnut compounds — but only about a third of people have the right gut bacteria to do this efficiently. The supplement skips that lottery.
cost_signal: $50–$80/month for branded Mitopure. Cheaper generic versions exist with less verification.
who_should_consider: Adults over 50 with declining endurance or sarcopenia. Anyone willing to pay for one of the most-evidenced longevity supplements.
who_should_skip: People who eat pomegranate and walnuts daily AND are confident converters (rare to know without a microbiome test).
verdict: Genuine mechanism, real human functional data, clean safety. Among the better-evidenced supplements. Pomegranate/walnut intake feeds the precursors but conversion is unreliable; the supplement bypasses microbiome variation.
what_to_do: Dose: 500–1000 mg/day. Branded version 'Mitopure' (Amazentis) is the studied form.
Take with food. Daily — not pulse.
Combine with strength training; the muscle-endurance benefit was strongest in active people.
Eating pomegranate seeds and walnuts gives you the precursors, but only ~40% of people have the gut bacteria to convert them. Cheaper, less reliable than the supplement.
No major safety concerns identified to date. Cleanest safety profile in this whole supplement section.
Reassess after 3 months — if you don't notice anything, the cost may not be worth it.

name: Vaccines (flu, shingles, pneumococcal)
one_liner: Annual flu vaccine reduces CV mortality 28% in recent MI patients (IAMI 2021). Shingles vaccine reduces dementia risk 20%. Among the most evidence-supported foundation interventions.
plain_summary: Vaccination is one of the highest-leverage longevity interventions there is, but it almost never appears on longevity-site lists because it isn't a supplement or a protocol. The flu vaccine, given annually, reduces cardiovascular mortality in the year following — the IAMI 2021 trial in recent MI patients showed 28% reduction in CV death and 41% reduction in all-cause death. The shingles vaccine reduces both the disease and (separately) dementia incidence — the natural-experiment data from Wales is one of the strongest pieces of evidence we have that infection contributes to neurodegeneration. Pneumococcal vaccination prevents pneumonia, which is one of the leading causes of death in older adults. None of these cost much; coverage is poor; the mortality benefit is comparable to taking a statin.
cost_signal: Free–$50/yr
who_should_consider: Every adult, especially 50+. Anyone with diabetes, cardiovascular disease, COPD, or immunosuppression has stronger relative benefit. Recent MI/heart surgery patients have especially strong indication for flu vaccine. Adults caring for or living with infants, elderly, or immunocompromised people.
who_should_skip: Severe allergic reaction to a previous dose of the same vaccine. Active acute illness with high fever (delay until recovered). Severe immunosuppression: live vaccines (e.g., live attenuated flu nasal spray, MMR, varicella) should be avoided; inactivated/recombinant vaccines (current flu shot, Shingrix, pneumococcal) are still safe. Specific contraindications: prior Guillain-Barré within 6 weeks of a flu vaccine warrants caution.
verdict: Foundation tier. Stack the vaccines you're eligible for. Annual flu shot has a CV mortality signal larger than most pills on this site. Shingles vaccine 50+. Pneumococcal sequence per current guidelines for 50-65+. RSV vaccine for 60+ if available. None require a doctor's appointment beyond a primary-care visit; most are free or under $50 even without insurance. The single most under-utilised foundation in the catalogue.
what_to_do: Annual flu vaccine — every year, ideally September-October before flu season. Standard-dose for under 65; high-dose or adjuvanted vaccine for 65+. Shingles vaccine (Shingrix, recombinant zoster) — 2-dose series at age 50+; very effective (>90%) and durable. Don't skip the second dose. Pneumococcal vaccines for 50+ (varies by guideline) — PCV20 single dose or PCV15 followed by PPSV23. Discuss schedule with primary care. RSV vaccine for 60+ where available (Arexvy, Abrysvo) — newer, growing evidence base. Tdap booster every 10 years (covers tetanus and pertussis). COVID-19 boosters per current guidelines.
contraindications: Severe allergic reaction to previous dose of same vaccine. Acute moderate-to-severe illness (delay). Severe immunosuppression: live vaccines avoided; inactivated/recombinant fine. History of Guillain-Barré within 6 weeks of prior flu vaccine warrants careful discussion.
side_effects: Local injection site soreness/redness (most common). Mild systemic symptoms 24-48h: fatigue, headache, low-grade fever. Severe reactions rare (anaphylaxis ~1 per million). Shingrix has more pronounced reactogenicity (fatigue, myalgia) — schedule second dose when you can rest the next day.

name: Vitamin B12
one_liner: Essential vitamin. Deficiency is common in older adults and vegans, causes irreversible neuro damage. Supplementation only matters if you're deficient.
plain_summary: Essential vitamin you can only get from animal foods (or supplements). Deficiency is common (10-30% of people over 65, and most strict vegans without supplements). Symptoms — fatigue, brain fog, tingling in hands/feet — can be subtle until permanent nerve damage occurs. Supplementing only matters if you're actually deficient; for everyone else, it's an expensive yellow stream in the toilet.
cost_signal: $5-10/month for generic methylcobalamin 1000 mcg. Sublingual or capsule equally effective.
who_should_consider: Vegan or strict vegetarian (mandatory — no plant source)
Age 60+ (atrophic gastritis common)
On metformin >2 years or PPIs long-term (both reduce absorption)
Unexplained fatigue, brain fog, or peripheral neuropathy
who_should_skip: You eat meat/fish/eggs/dairy regularly and have no symptoms — diet covers it
You're chasing energy from B12 shots — placebo if you're not deficient
verdict: Critical if you're deficient — and you might be without knowing. Test before supplementing if you're unsure (serum B12, methylmalonic acid). Mandatory for vegans. Don't take 'energy' shots — those are placebos for non-deficient people. The CV-prevention story didn't hold up in trials.
what_to_do: First: test if you might be deficient. Get serum B12 and methylmalonic acid (MMA — more sensitive). Test if: you're 60+, vegan/vegetarian, on metformin or PPIs long-term, have unexplained fatigue/numbness, or have macrocytic anemia. If deficient: 1000 mcg/day sublingual or oral for 8 weeks, then 1000 mcg/week or 2500 mcg twice weekly maintenance. If vegan but not deficient: 250-500 mcg/day or B12-fortified foods.
side_effects: Generally none — water soluble, excess excreted in urine
Acne flare in some users at high doses

name: Vitamin C
one_liner: Essential vitamin. Strong evidence for preventing scurvy. Modest evidence for cold-duration shortening. Megadoses do nothing extra.
plain_summary: Essential vitamin you need to prevent scurvy (a few hundred years ago this killed sailors). For modern non-deficient people: small evidence it shortens colds by about 8% if taken regularly. Major heart-disease and cancer claims have failed in trials. Diet from oranges, peppers, broccoli covers everyone — supplements add little.
cost_signal: $3-8/month for generic vitamin C 500mg. Time-released or buffered slightly more.
who_should_consider: You don't eat fruits/vegetables regularly
Heavy training (marathon, military training) — preventive effect real here
Smoker (higher requirement, ~35mg/day extra)
You catch colds often
who_should_skip: You eat 2+ servings of vitamin-C foods daily
You believe in megadoses (10g+) — no benefit, kidney stone risk
IV vitamin C clinics — pseudoscience for non-cancer use
verdict: Get from food (citrus, peppers, broccoli). RDA of 90mg covers everyone non-deficient. Supplements at 200-500mg might shorten colds slightly. Pauling-style megadoses (10g+) do nothing useful and increase kidney stone risk in predisposed people. Skip the IV vitamin C clinics — total theater.
what_to_do: Eat citrus, peppers, broccoli, kiwi daily — covers most people. If supplementing: 200-500 mg/day. Higher doses don't help (your blood plateaus and you pee out the rest). Doubling the dose during a cold is reasonable — small evidence it shortens duration.
contraindications: History of calcium oxalate kidney stones (use lower doses)
side_effects: Increased oxalate excretion (kidney stone risk in predisposed)

name: Vitamin D3
one_liner: Replete deficiency, yes. Supplement above sufficiency for longevity? Marginal.
plain_summary: If your vitamin D is low, fix it — being deficient is bad. But if your level is already normal, taking more does almost nothing for longevity, despite what the supplement industry implies. Test your blood level first. Don't just assume.
cost_signal: $3–$10/month for a basic D3 supplement. Test costs $30–$80 once or twice a year.
who_should_consider: Anyone living above 40° latitude (most of Europe, northern US/Canada), dark-skinned individuals, indoor workers, older adults, anyone testing below 30 ng/mL.
who_should_skip: People already in the 30–50 ng/mL range. People with sarcoidosis, hyperparathyroidism, or kidney stones (talk to your doctor).
verdict: Test your level. If deficient, replete to sufficiency. The data does NOT support stacking high-dose D for longevity above sufficiency. K2 pairing for arterial Ca is plausible but unproven.
what_to_do: Get a 25(OH)D blood test. Order it yourself if needed (many direct-to-consumer labs offer it for $30–50).
Target range: 30–50 ng/mL (75–125 nmol/L). Below 20 ng/mL is deficient.
If deficient: 2000–4000 IU/day vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol, not D2) for 8–12 weeks, then retest.
If sufficient: a daily 1000 IU is fine but unnecessary if you get sun exposure or eat fatty fish.
Take with a fat-containing meal (it's fat-soluble).
Don't megadose 'just in case'. Above ~5000 IU/day chronically can elevate calcium and harm kidneys/arteries.
side_effects: Hypercalcemia at high doses (>10,000 IU/day chronic)

name: Vitamin K2 (MK-7, MK-4)
one_liner: Theoretical role in vascular calcification reduction. Strong cohort data, weak RCT data.
plain_summary: Vitamin K2 (different from the K1 in spinach) helps direct calcium to your bones instead of letting it deposit in your arteries — at least that's the theory. Cohort data looks good, but the gold-standard randomized trials are weak. Cheap hedge if you're already supplementing vitamin D. Don't take it if you're on a blood thinner like warfarin — it interferes with the medication.
who_should_consider: People supplementing vitamin D3 for extra hedge. People with low bone density. People with a coronary calcium score above zero who want a low-cost layer.
who_should_skip: Anyone on warfarin. People who already eat natto/aged cheese regularly.
verdict: Cheap hedge with plausible mechanism, weak RCT support. Pair with D3 if you supplement. Eat natto if you can stand it; else 100-200μg MK-7 supplement.
what_to_do: Best: eat the food sources — natto (if you can stand it), hard aged cheese, egg yolks, grass-fed butter.
If supplementing: 100–200 μg/day of MK-7 form (longer half-life, daily dosing works) or MK-4 (shorter half-life, multiple daily doses).
Pair with vitamin D3. Many supplements combine them; that's fine.
Take with a meal containing fat (it's fat-soluble).
DO NOT take K2 if you're on warfarin (Coumadin). It interferes directly with how the drug works.
Newer blood thinners (apixaban, rivaroxaban) don't have this interaction — but tell your doctor anyway.

name: VO2max Training
one_liner: High-intensity intervals push the aerobic ceiling. Strongest single fitness-mortality lever.
plain_summary: VO2max — the maximum oxygen your body can use during hard exercise — is one of the most predictive numbers we have for how long you'll live. The way to raise it is short, very hard intervals: roughly 4 minutes near-max effort, 4 minutes recovery, 4 rounds. Once a week on top of your easy cardio is enough to move the needle.
cost_signal: $0–$50/month gym. Heart rate monitor $50 one-time. VO2max test $100–$300 if you want a baseline.
who_should_consider: Healthy adults willing to suffer briefly for outsized return. Anyone whose VO2max tests below average for their age (any decent fitness lab can measure it for ~$100–200).
who_should_skip: Active cardiac disease without clearance, recent injury, untreated severe hypertension, or anyone unable to tolerate near-max effort safely.
verdict: Highest-effect-per-hour modality for longevity in any modality, anywhere. One 4×4 session/week, plus Zone 2 base, plus strength training = the canonical longevity exercise prescription.
what_to_do: Once or twice per week, do 4×4 intervals: 4 min hard, 4 min easy, repeat 4 times. Total ~32 min including warmup/cooldown.
'Hard' = 90–95% of max heart rate. You shouldn't be able to talk in full sentences. RPE 8–9 out of 10.
Pick something that lets you control intensity: stationary bike, rower, treadmill at incline, hill repeats. Avoid technical activities for the hard intervals.
Warm up properly: 10 min easy first. Cool down 5 min easy after. Skipping these raises injury risk.
Pair with 3–4 hours/week of Zone 2 cardio + 2–3 strength sessions. This is the canonical longevity prescription.
Don't add more than 1–2 VO2max sessions/week. The recovery cost is real.
If you have known cardiac disease, get a stress test or doctor clearance before going near max effort.

name: Walnuts and Tree Nuts
one_liner: Daily nut consumption is one of the strongest food-based mortality signals in epidemiology. PREDIMED-NUTS RCT showed ~28% CV-event reduction with ~30g/day mixed nuts.
plain_summary: Eating a small handful of nuts (about 30g) every day is one of the most consistent dietary patterns linked to lower mortality in epidemiological studies, and the PREDIMED trial — the biggest randomized diet trial ever — showed mixed nuts plus a Mediterranean diet cut major heart events by 28% over 5 years. Walnuts specifically have evidence for protecting cognition in older adults. About as evidence-based as food gets.
cost_signal: $15-30/month for ~30g/day of bulk raw walnuts/almonds/mixed nuts. Costco bulk bags are best value.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone — easiest evidence-based dietary upgrade
Elevated LDL or family history of CVD
Older adults wanting cognitive support (walnuts specifically)
Don't currently eat nuts
who_should_skip: Tree nut allergy — substitute seeds (pumpkin, sunflower) for similar profile
Calorie restriction with no room to add 200 kcal
Eating sugared/candied nuts — those don't count
verdict: One of the best-evidenced food choices in the longevity literature. ~30g/day (a small handful) of mixed nuts. Walnuts specifically have the most cognitive evidence (WAHA trial in elderly). Don't bother with 'walnut oil' supplements — eat the actual nuts. Watch portion size — calories add up fast (180 kcal per 28g serving).
what_to_do: Eat 28-30 g (a small handful) of mixed nuts most days. Walnuts daily for cognitive evidence (WAHA). Almonds for LDL evidence (multiple trials). Pistachios for satiety. Mix is fine. Eat them as a snack or topping (yogurt, salad), not as candied/sugared 'nut bars'. Raw or dry-roasted, no salt. Watch portion — measure once with a kitchen scale to calibrate eye.
side_effects: Mild GI distress at very high intake (>60g/day)

name: Whey Protein
one_liner: The most-studied protein source for muscle synthesis. Most useful for older adults who under-eat protein and people who train.
plain_summary: Whey protein is the fast-absorbing protein fraction from milk. Per gram, it has the highest concentration of leucine — the amino acid that triggers muscle protein synthesis. For most people eating an adequate, varied diet, regular food covers protein needs. The exceptions where whey shines: older adults who can't easily eat 30g protein at every meal (sarcopenia prevention), people who train and want to recover faster, vegetarians who eat dairy but not enough protein-dense foods, and anyone trying to gain muscle. Not magical — just convenient and well-studied.
who_should_consider: Older adults (60+) experiencing sarcopenia or with low total protein intake. People doing resistance training. Vegetarians who eat dairy. Anyone struggling to hit ≥1.6g protein/kg/day from food. Pre/post bariatric surgery (where dense protein matters and intake volume is limited).
who_should_skip: Lactose intolerance (use whey isolate, which has minimal lactose). Milk protein allergy. Stage 4-5 CKD without dialysis (protein restriction matters). People meeting protein needs from whole foods. Pure vegan diets (use plant-protein blends instead — pea+rice approaches whey's amino acid profile).
verdict: If you're 60+ and your protein intake is variable, 30g whey at one meal is a cheap insurance policy. If you train resistance, 20-40g post-workout is the standard recommendation backed by meta-analysis. If you eat ≥1.6g/kg/day from food, you're already covered — no need to add. Plant proteins (pea+rice) work nearly as well at slightly higher doses.
what_to_do: 20-40g whey protein within 2 hours of resistance training, OR 25-30g at any meal where you're under-protein. Whey isolate (>90% protein, low lactose) better tolerated than concentrate; concentrate is cheaper. Mix with water, milk, or smoothie. Most people don't need it daily if eating ≥1.6g/kg/day from food. Older adults (60+): 30g whey at one meal can reliably hit the leucine threshold even when whole-food intake is variable.
contraindications: Milk protein allergy. Stage 4-5 CKD without dialysis. Pre-existing severe lactose intolerance (with concentrate; isolate is fine).
side_effects: GI symptoms in lactose-intolerant individuals (use isolate). Acne reports in susceptible individuals (small but real). High doses may cause GI distress.

name: Zinc
one_liner: Essential mineral. Strong evidence for shortening colds when taken within 24h of symptoms. Long-term high doses cause copper deficiency.
plain_summary: Essential mineral your immune system needs. Best evidence: zinc acetate lozenges started within 24 hours of cold symptoms cut cold duration by about 33%. For older adults with subclinical zinc deficiency, daily supplementation reduces infections. Important warning: high doses long-term cause copper deficiency, which causes anemia and nerve problems.
cost_signal: $5-10/month for generic zinc 15-30mg. Zinc acetate lozenges (Cold-EEZE etc.) $10-20 per cold-season supply.
who_should_consider: Older adult with frequent infections
Heavy training or recovery from injury
Vegetarian/vegan with limited zinc-rich foods
Cold lozenges at first sign of cold symptoms (specifically zinc acetate)
who_should_skip: You eat meat, shellfish, or pumpkin seeds regularly — diet covers it
You're already taking 50+mg/day long-term — risk of copper deficiency
Zinc gluconate nasal sprays — caused permanent loss of smell in some users (FDA warning)
verdict: RDA dose (8-11mg) handled by diet for most people (meat, shellfish, beans). For colds: zinc acetate lozenges 80mg/day starting at symptom onset — evidence-based. Don't take 50+mg long-term unless you also supplement copper. Older adults with frequent infections may benefit from 15-30mg/day.
what_to_do: For prevention/maintenance: 8-15 mg/day with food (or just eat oysters, beef, pumpkin seeds). For colds: 80 mg/day elemental zinc as zinc acetate lozenges, started within 24 hours of first symptoms, 6-8 lozenges daily, for max 5-7 days. Don't take >40 mg/day long-term without copper supplementation.
contraindications: Long-term high doses without copper supplementation
side_effects: Long-term high dose causes copper deficiency

name: Zone 2 Cardio
one_liner: Sub-lactate-threshold steady-state cardio. The mitochondrial-density driver.
plain_summary: Easy steady-state cardio — the kind where you can hold a conversation but you're working — is probably the single highest-leverage thing you can do for longevity besides not smoking. It builds the cellular machinery (mitochondria) that keeps your heart, brain, and metabolism young. 3–4 hours a week beats anything in a pill bottle.
cost_signal: $0 if you walk. $50 for a heart-rate strap. $30–$80/month if you use a gym for the bike/rower.
who_should_consider: Almost everyone. The lowest barrier, highest payoff item on this site.
who_should_skip: No one. People with cardiac conditions should see a doctor for clearance, not skip it.
verdict: Probably the highest-leverage time investment for longevity besides not smoking. 3-4 hours/week of Zone 2 (e.g., brisk hike, easy bike) plus 1 VO2max session beats anything in a pill bottle.
what_to_do: Pick something you'll actually do: brisk hike, easy bike, rower, elliptical, jog (if you can stay in zone). Walking on hills counts.
Find your zone: pace where you can speak in full sentences with mild effort but not sing. Heart rate roughly 60–70% of max (180 minus your age is a rough guide).
Aim for 3–4 hours/week minimum. Better: 4–5 hours. Spread across 3–5 sessions of 45–60 min each.
Add ONE VO2max session per week: 4 x 4 minutes at near-maximal effort with 4-minute rests. This is the second-biggest mortality lever.
Don't go too hard. The most common mistake is drifting into Zone 3 — feels productive but skips the Zone 2 adaptation.
Get a basic chest-strap heart rate monitor ($50). Wrist optical HR is unreliable for this.
